entries.prompt: Walk me through a moment from the last few days that's still replaying in your head — what happened, who was there, and what you keep coming back to about it.
entries.content:
Tuesday meeting. The one where Drew asked, kind of out of nowhere, "Kai what do you think?" and I had a whole second of dead air before I said anything. Maybe two seconds. It felt like ten. I had been half-listening because I was watching Reese's face to see if Reese understood the thing being discussed, which is its own embarrassing fact about me. And then suddenly the question was mine and I said something like, "Yeah I think the second option makes more sense, just because of the timeline." Which — fine. It was fine. It wasn't wrong. Drew nodded and moved on. Nobody died.

But I keep playing it back. The pause. The way my voice came out a little high. The fact that I said "just because of the timeline" instead of actually explaining anything, like I was trying to land on the answer with the fewest possible words so I could stop talking. Drew's face didn't change. That's what's bothering me, I think. Drew didn't react. Which means either it was a fine answer and I'm doing this to myself, or it was a forgettable answer and I'm being correctly forgotten. I genuinely cannot tell which one is worse to me right now.

Why does this keep happening. I had things to say. I had been thinking about the timeline thing for a week, I had actual reasons, I had a whole little version of this in my head where I said something specific about the dependency on the other team and Drew went "huh, good catch." That version did not happen. The version that happened was me saying twelve words and then sitting there with my heart loud.

After the meeting I went and got coffee from the kitchen and Reese was in there and said something nice about the meeting, like "good call on the timeline thing" and I said "thanks" and immediately wanted to crawl out of my skin because was that sarcastic? Was Reese being nice or was Reese being kind in the way you're kind to a dog that almost got hit by a car. I don't think Reese is like that. I don't have evidence Reese is like that. But the brain went there immediately and I had to walk back to my desk pretending my face wasn't doing something.

I told Avery about it that night, sort of. I said, "I had a weird moment in a meeting today" and Avery said "weird how" and then I downplayed it because I could hear how it would sound out loud. I said, "I just felt like I gave a lame answer to a question." And Avery said, "did anyone say it was a lame answer?" and I said no, and Avery said, "then probably it was fine, babe." And I wanted that to land. I really did. But it skidded off. Because Avery doesn't know what it's like in the meeting. Avery doesn't know that Drew is the person whose opinion is somehow weighing like four hundred pounds in my chest right now. And I didn't want to explain it because explaining it would make me sound like the kind of person who takes a two-second pause in a meeting and turns it into a whole thing, which is, apparently, the kind of person I am.

We watched something on the couch after. Avery's hand on my knee. I was there but I wasn't, I was still in the meeting, I was rewriting what I should have said. The actual good answer. The one with the dependency point. Why didn't I say it. I knew it. It was right there. There's something humiliating about knowing the thing and not saying the thing, more humiliating than not knowing it.

The thing I keep coming back to, actually, is that Drew asked me. That's the part that won't let go. Drew picked me. Out of the people in the room. Which means Drew thought I might have something to say, or wanted to give me a chance to say something, or — okay maybe I'm reading too much in. Maybe Drew was just going around the room. But it didn't feel like going around the room. It felt specific. And I — what — gave a twelve-word answer and sat down. Metaphorically. I was already sitting.

I don't know. There's a version of this where I tell myself it was fine, the answer was fine, Drew moved on, nobody is thinking about it except me, and that's probably true and also doesn't help. Because the part that's stuck isn't whether the answer was fine. It's the picture of myself I had walking into that meeting versus the picture I had walking out. Walking in I was someone who had been thinking carefully about the project. Walking out I was someone who choked a little when called on. And those two people are the same person, I know they are, but they don't feel the same and I want to be the first one and I'm scared I'm always going to be the second one when it counts.

It's almost eleven. The plant on the windowsill looks worse than it did last week. I should water it. I keep forgetting. I'll water it tomorrow. Or I won't. I'll go to bed and tomorrow I'll go to work and Drew will say good morning or won't and I'll spend the whole day reading the tea leaves of it. God. Okay. Going to bed.

entries.prompt: Think about someone whose opinion of you sits in your head more than it should. When did you last catch yourself adjusting around them — what did you do differently, and what were you hoping they'd think?
entries.content:
Drew. Obviously Drew. I don't even need the prompt to know.

Yesterday, like 3pm, Drew sent a Slack that just said "got a sec?" and I felt my whole stomach drop out before I'd even read past the second word. Three words. Got a sec. I sat there for a full breath staring at it trying to decide if the lowercase G meant something. Like, is this a casual got-a-sec or is this a "we need to talk" got-a-sec dressed up as casual. I typed "yep!" and then deleted the exclamation point because too eager and then put it back because no exclamation point seemed cold and then took it off again and just sent "yep, what's up" which I then immediately regretted because "what's up" is what you say to a friend, not your manager, and now I sound like I don't take things seriously.

It was nothing. Drew just wanted to know if I'd had a chance to look at a doc. I said I'd seen it but hadn't gone deep yet, which was true, and Drew said "no rush, just wanted to check." And then we were done. Forty seconds. Maybe less.

But here's the thing. Before I replied I'd already opened the doc in another tab, scrolled through it really fast, and tried to manufacture a take in case Drew asked. I had a little sentence ready in my head about the second section. I was loading the gun before I knew if there was a gun. And then Drew didn't ask and I had this stupid take just sitting there in my mouth and I almost — I almost said it anyway. Like, "by the way, the second section, I was thinking..." Just to land it. Just to make sure Drew knew I'd been thinking. I caught myself. I just said okay sounds good. But the wanting was so strong it almost made me do it.

That's the adjusting. That's what I do around Drew. I rehearse. I pre-load. I scan messages for tone like I'm decoding something. I walk into one-on-ones with three little prepared anecdotes about what I've been working on, casual-sounding, like oh by the way, as if I haven't been polishing them on the bus.

What am I hoping Drew thinks. That's the question and I don't love the answer. I'm hoping Drew thinks I'm sharp. I'm hoping Drew thinks I'm one of the good ones, whatever that means. I want Drew to say something, eventually, like "you've really stepped up" or "I'm glad we hired you" or — god this is embarrassing to write — to mention me to someone else in a way that gets back to me. I want to be talked about favorably when I'm not in the room. That's it. That's the whole thing. I want Drew to like me in the specific way where Drew thinks I'm competent and a little bit impressive and worth investing in.

And I know — I KNOW — that this is too much weight to put on one person. I knew it when I started typing this. Drew is a manager. Drew has a whole life and a whole set of priorities and is probably thinking about me approximately 4% as much as I am thinking about Drew thinking about me. Drew is not auditing my Slack punctuation. Drew sent "got a sec" and meant got a sec.

But the thing is when I imagine Drew NOT thinking I'm good at this job, when I imagine Drew being secretly disappointed, the floor kind of falls out. Like a real physical drop. And I don't have a good reason for that other than. Drew is the person who decides if I'm doing this right. And I don't know yet if I'm doing it right. And until I know I'm going to keep checking the mirror that is Drew's face every five minutes to see what I look like.

The other thing I noticed yesterday — after the Slack thing I went and got water and on the way back I passed Drew's desk and Drew was laughing at something on the screen and I felt this weird pang. Not jealousy exactly. More like — I want to be the thing on the screen that's making Drew laugh. I want to be the one Drew is enjoying. Which is so much. That's so much to want from your manager. That's not what managers are for. I think I know that. But I want it anyway.

Avery would say, if I told Avery this, "you don't need Drew to like you, you need Drew to think you're doing your job." And Avery would be right. And I would nod. And I would still walk into work tomorrow and check Drew's Slack status and try to read whether the away light means something.

I think part of what's happening is — okay, in college there were a lot of mirrors. Professors, friends, the group, all reflecting some version of me back. I knew where I stood because there were a hundred small signals every day. Now there's basically Drew. Drew is the one signal. Of course I'm staring at it. Of course every flicker feels huge. It's the only light in the room.

I should probably find more lights. Reese is nice, actually, and I keep half-flinching away from Reese because I'm scared of the comparison thing, but Reese might just be a person who would also tell me if I was doing okay. Other people on the team. Anyone. It cannot just be Drew.

Plant still looks bad. I did water it this time. Probably too late. We'll see.

entries.prompt: When was the last time you felt genuinely at ease around someone — not performing, not scanning their face for a reaction? Describe what you were doing and what made it different.
entries.content:
Sunday morning. Avery and I went to the coffee place — the one where the barista with the septum ring knows my order, knows I always almost get the oat milk thing and then chicken out and get regular. I didn't chicken out this time. Got the oat milk thing. It was fine. That's not the moment though.

The moment was after, walking back. We didn't have anywhere to be. That's maybe the whole thing right there. We didn't have anywhere to be. It was that weird soft Sunday where the city is half-asleep and people are out with dogs and nobody is in a hurry, and Avery was telling me this long stupid story about a coworker who keeps bringing the same tupperware to the office and forgetting it in the fridge over the weekend, and I was laughing in that way where you're not performing the laugh, it's just coming out. I had coffee in one hand and Avery's hand in the other and the sun was doing the thing where it cuts between buildings and lands in stripes on the sidewalk. And I realized, walking, that I hadn't checked my phone in maybe an hour. Which for me is — yeah.

What was different. I think it's that Avery wasn't asking me anything. That's part of it. Avery was just talking. There was no question I needed to answer correctly. There was no face I was reading. I mean Avery's face was right there but I wasn't scanning it, I was just looking at it, which is a different thing and I don't usually notice the difference until I'm doing the not-scanning version. Avery's face when telling a story is so — Avery commits to the bit. Avery does the voices. There was a part where Avery did the coworker's voice and I laughed so hard I had to stop walking for a second.

And the other thing. I wasn't thinking about what I looked like. Walking next to Avery I wasn't running the tape of how I was coming across. I was just there. In the body. Coffee, hand, sidewalk, sun. I keep wanting to figure out what specifically made that possible because I want to bottle it. But I think the answer is dumb and simple which is that Avery has known me for eight months and has seen me be weird and tired and bad at things and is still here, so the audition is, at least with Avery, kind of over. Or it should be. I still audition sometimes. But less. On a Sunday morning with no plans, basically not at all.

We stopped at the little park, the one with the one bench that's always wet for some reason, and we didn't sit, we just stood there for a minute looking at a dog that was losing its mind over a squirrel. I said something like "that's the most alive anyone has been all week" and Avery laughed and said "you, or the dog" and I said "the dog, obviously" and it was — that was a good exchange. It was nothing. It was the kind of nothing that makes a relationship.

I want to write this part carefully because it's the part I keep skating past. There was a second on the walk back where I felt this little flicker of — what — guilt? Almost. Like, you don't deserve this. Like, Avery is being so easy and so present and you're going to mess it up somehow, you're going to be too much later this week, you're going to wake up tomorrow and be tightly wound about Drew again and Avery is going to have to deal with that version of you. And I noticed the flicker and then I — I don't know, I just kept walking. I didn't fix it. I didn't do anything with it. I just held Avery's hand and kept walking and the flicker passed. Which felt like a small win. Usually the flicker hooks me and pulls me out of wherever I am. This time it didn't.

I think what I'm circling around is that ease, for me, seems to require an absence of stakes. Or the feeling of an absence. Sunday morning, no plans, nobody evaluating anything — fine, I can be a person. The second the stakes come back, even imaginary ones, I'm scanning again. Which is depressing in one way because it means most of my life right now feels stakes-y. But it's also useful information because it means the not-scanning version of me exists, I have evidence, I was just walking around in it for two hours on Sunday.

Other things from the weekend that had this quality, smaller — making the bed together without talking. Avery put on music and we were just doing the corners. That was nice. And later, watching Avery cook, which I do sometimes when I don't want to be helpful I just want to watch, and Avery doesn't mind being watched. Avery cooks like someone who isn't being graded. I'd like to do anything the way Avery cooks.

It's funny because I came into this entry thinking the answer was going to be some specific moment with a specific punchline and actually the answer is just: a Sunday. A whole Sunday where my shoulders weren't up around my ears. The barista said the thing about the oat milk being a good choice and I said "we'll see, I'm a coward usually" and the barista laughed and that was easy too. Easy with strangers, easy with Avery, easy with myself for a few hours.

Tomorrow is Monday and the shoulders will go back up. I know they will. But it was nice to remember they come down.

Plant is still alive, by the way. Surprised me.

entries.prompt: There's a version of you that shows up at work and a version that shows up on a Sunday walk with Avery. Describe a recent moment when you felt the gap between them — when you wished you could bring some of one into the other, or were relieved you couldn't.
entries.content:
Thursday. Team lunch thing. Drew had organized it, one of those quarterly we-should-do-this-more lunches where everyone goes to the place down the block and orders bowls and tries to be casual. I sat down across from Reese and two seats away from Drew and immediately felt my whole body recalibrate. Like a dial turning. Shoulders up. The little voice going, okay, what's the move here, are you going to be funny, are you going to be thoughtful, pick a lane.

Someone — I think it was Mara from the other team — asked what everyone did over the weekend. And I had this whole real answer. I had the Sunday. I had Avery doing the coworker's voice and the dog losing its mind and the oat milk thing. I had a story. And what came out of my mouth was, "Oh, not much, kind of a chill one, you?" Pivot. Deflect. Like I was guarding state secrets. Like the truth — that I had a really nice weekend with my partner and laughed at a dog — was somehow too soft to put on the table. And then Reese said something about a hike and Drew said something about a kid's birthday party and I sat there with my fork in a bowl wondering why I couldn't just be a person who had a nice weekend.

That's the gap. Right there. Sunday me would've told the dog story. Sunday me does voices, apparently, when it's just Avery. Work me edited the dog story out of existence in like half a second because Sunday me felt too — I don't know — too unguarded? Too small? I think I was scared the story would land flat. Or worse, that Drew would smile politely and I'd feel the smile being polite and spend the rest of lunch convinced I'd misjudged the room.

The wild thing is I don't think anyone would have cared. People tell dog stories at lunch. That's like 60% of what lunch is. But there's some filter on between Sunday me and work me where any detail that feels too much like my actual life gets stripped out, in case it makes me look — what. Unserious. Soft. Like someone whose weekends are just walks. Which is, again, what most people's weekends are.

Reese, by the way, told a story about getting lost on the hike and ending up at a gas station eating a hot dog at 4pm, and everyone laughed, and it was such an easy story, and I thought — Reese just brought Sunday Reese to the table. And nothing bad happened. Drew laughed. Mara laughed. Reese just got to be a person who eats a gas station hot dog. I want that. I want to be able to do that. I had it on Sunday. I had it walking down the sidewalk with Avery's hand in mine. Where does it go between there and the bowl place on Thursday.

Okay but here's the other side of it. Walking back to the office after lunch, I felt this little — relief, almost — that I hadn't told the dog story. Because the dog story is mine. It's mine and Avery's. And there's a part of me that didn't want to take that Sunday and flatten it into a thirty-second anecdote for a table of coworkers who would have nodded and moved on. Like the version of the story that survives at that table wouldn't have been the actual thing. The actual thing was Avery's voice doing the coworker and the sun in stripes and my shoulders being down. None of that survives "so what'd you do this weekend." It just becomes "took a walk, got coffee, it was nice." Which is true and also nothing.

So maybe some of the gap is on purpose. Maybe I'm protecting something. The Sunday version of me doesn't necessarily want to be on display at the bowl place. The Sunday version of me wants to be at the park looking at a dog. Those might just be different rooms and that might be fine.

But then I think — no, that's letting myself off too easy. Because it wasn't just the dog story. It was that the whole time at lunch I was doing the thing. Tracking Drew's face. Trying to figure out when to say something funny. I had a half-decent line about the bowl place's music and I chickened on it because I wasn't sure if the line was funny or just weird. Sunday me said "the dog is the most alive anyone has been all week" without thinking and Avery laughed. Thursday me sat on a perfectly fine line about a Sade song playing in a place that sells quinoa because I was scared it would land at 6 out of 10 instead of 8.

That's what I want to bring across. Not the dog story specifically. The willingness to just say the thing and see what happens. The not-rehearsing. Sunday me doesn't rehearse because there's no audition. Thursday me is auditioning constantly, even at lunch, even when no one is grading.

And I guess what I'm realizing, writing this, is that the audition isn't actually being run by Drew or Reese or Mara. It's being run by me. They were just eating bowls. I was the one with the clipboard.

I got back to my desk and I had a Slack from Avery — just a picture of our plant, with the caption "she lives." And I laughed out loud at my desk, real laugh, before I could censor it. And for a second the two versions touched. Sunday me leaked into Thursday me for about four seconds and then I got self-conscious about having laughed audibly and it closed back up.

But four seconds. That's something. The plant is alive and so, occasionally, am I, even at work.

entries.prompt: When was the last time you felt small in a way that surprised you — not in a bad situation, just a regular moment where something shrunk you? Walk me through what happened and what was running underneath it.
entries.content:
Tuesday night. I was on the phone with Mom. Regular call, the Tuesday call, she always calls Tuesday because that's when Dad has his thing and she's alone in the house and a little bored. Nothing was wrong. That's the part I want to underline. Nothing was wrong.

She was telling me about the neighbors' kid, the one I went to middle school with — Jamie? Janie? — who apparently just got engaged and bought a house, and Mom was telling me this in the voice she uses when she's not making a point but is also definitely making a point. "They got such a good rate, can you believe it." And I made the noises. Mm. Wow. That's nice. And then she said, totally casually, "are you putting anything away each month? Like into savings?"

And I went small. Right there. On the bus, on the way home, with my headphones in. I felt my whole self get tiny.

Because the answer is — kind of? I have the automatic thing that pulls a little out every paycheck, which I set up when I started, and I haven't looked at it in months, and I don't actually know how much is in there. And I have no idea if it's the right amount. I have no idea what the right amount even is. And I told her, "yeah, I have a thing set up," and she said "oh good, sweetie, that's smart," and that should have been the end of it. She moved on. She started talking about Dad's thing. But I was gone. I was just gone for the rest of the call. Nodding into the phone. Saying yeah. Saying mm-hm.

What was running underneath. Okay. It wasn't even about the money exactly. It was that for one second I saw myself the way she might see me. Twenty-four. Apartment with a dying plant, except now the plant is alive, fine, but the apartment is still the apartment. Takeout containers in the trash. A job I can't really explain to her, doing things she doesn't understand, in a city she's only visited once. Eight months into a relationship she's met Avery exactly twice via FaceTime. And she's asking, very gently, the kind of question a parent asks when they're trying to find out if you're okay without asking if you're okay.

And the small feeling was — I don't know if I am. Okay in the way she means. Okay in the way where you have a plan, where you know what next year looks like, where you're putting the right amount away each month because you know what you're putting it away for. Jamie or Janie has a house. I have a Slack channel where I check my manager's away light.

I got off the bus and I was almost home and I stopped at the bodega for no reason. Just to be somewhere with fluorescent lights and not be inside my apartment yet. Bought a seltzer I didn't want. The guy at the counter said "long day?" and I said "yeah" and I think my face must have looked like something because he gave me the seltzer for free. He waved me off. He said, "on me, kid." Kid. He called me kid. He's maybe five years older than me, max. And I got out of there and walked the rest of the block holding this free seltzer feeling about nine years old.

That's the surprising part. The bodega guy made me feel like a child more than my mother did. My mother was being normal. The bodega guy was being kind. And the kindness undid me a little because I think I'd been holding something the whole call and didn't know it until someone was nice to me for free.

What was running underneath, really, all the way underneath — I think it's that I'm twenty-four and I keep waiting to feel like an adult and I don't. I keep waiting for the part where the apartment feels like mine, where the job feels like a thing I do instead of a thing I'm getting away with, where my mother asks about savings and I have a confident answer. And instead I just keep — accumulating. Months. A relationship. A plant. A barista who knows my order. None of it feels like it's adding up to a person yet. It feels like I'm wearing the costume of someone who has a life and the costume mostly fits but every so often someone looks at me a certain way and I remember it's a costume.

Mom didn't do anything wrong. That's the thing I keep coming back to. She asked a normal question. The smallness came from inside. It came from the gap between the version of me she thinks she's calling and the version of me actually on the bus. She's calling someone who has a job in a big city and a serious-feeling relationship and a savings account. That person exists, technically. I am that person, technically. But on the inside that person is also someone who almost cried at a free seltzer.

Got home, Avery wasn't over, just me. I sat on the couch with the seltzer and didn't open it for a while. Eventually opened it. Ordered Thai food. The fuck-it voice came in right on schedule, around 9. I let it. I watched something dumb. Texted Avery a heart, no words. Avery sent one back.

I think the thing I want to say, before I close this, is that the smallness wasn't even bad exactly. It was almost — clarifying? Like for a second I saw the whole scaffolding of my life from outside and saw how thin it is. And that was scary. But also it's actually how thin it is, and it was sort of useful to see. I'd been walking around assuming the scaffolding was thicker. It's not. It's just months and a plant and some people who are nice to me. That might be all anyone's is. I don't know. I'm twenty-four. I genuinely don't know.

The seltzer was lime. I don't even like lime. I drank it anyway.

entries.prompt: Tell me about something you've been meaning to look into or sort out — the kind of thing that sits on a mental list and never quite gets touched. What is it, and what happens in the moment you decide, again, not to deal with it?
entries.content:
The savings thing. Obviously the savings thing. It's been sitting there since the call with Mom and I have done exactly nothing about it.

Here's what "looking into it" would actually involve. Open the app. Log in — I don't remember the password, so reset the password, get the email, click the link, make a new password that's not the same as the old one because they don't let you, write it down somewhere or don't and just go through this again in three months. See the number. Look at the percentage I'm putting in. Decide if it's the right percentage. Which means knowing what the right percentage is, which means googling, which means reading some article that says "by 25 you should have" and then quietly closing the tab. Maybe also looking at the other thing, the retirement thing from work, which I set up in like ten minutes during onboarding while someone was waiting for me to finish so we could go to lunch. I picked a fund based on the year I'd be sixty-something. I have no idea what's in it. I don't know what any of the words on that page mean. Target date. Allocation. Expense ratio. I nodded at all of them like I knew what they were and clicked continue.

So that's the task. Maybe forty minutes if I'm honest. Maybe an hour. Not a huge thing. And it's been on the list since — god, since I started the job, basically. Two years. I have been "going to look at it" for two years.

What happens in the moment I decide not to deal with it. Okay. Last night, this is fresh. I was on the couch, laptop open, about to do it. I'd even told myself, after dinner, I'm going to do the savings thing. I had the tab open. The login screen. And I sat there and looked at it and then I — opened a new tab. Just casually. Like I wasn't doing what I was doing. New tab, and then I went to look at something on Instagram, just for a second, and then I was watching a video of someone making a sandwich, and then another video, and then it was forty minutes later and I closed the laptop and went to bed.

The decision happened so fast I almost didn't catch it. It wasn't "I'm not going to do this." It was "I'll do it in a minute, let me just —" and then the minute eats the night. There's a tiny flinch right at the moment of opening the login screen, like the page itself is bright and loud, and then my hand just goes somewhere else. New tab. Phone. Anything. The flinch is so small I barely notice it, but it's there, and it's the whole game.

What's underneath the flinch. I think it's that the second I look at the number, the number is real. Right now the number is whatever it is. It's Schrödinger's number. It might be fine. It might be embarrassing. It might be enough that I'd feel okay, or it might be so little that I'd feel like an idiot for not having paid attention. As long as I don't look, it can be both. The not-looking is actually a kind of comfort. It's the savings equivalent of not weighing yourself.

And there's the other layer, which is — I don't trust myself to know what to do with the information once I have it. Like, say I open it and the number is small. Then what. Do I increase the contribution? By how much? Based on what? I don't have a budget, not a real one. I have a vague sense that takeout is too much and that's about as far as it goes. So opening the app isn't just opening the app. It's opening the app and then realizing I should also have a budget, and then realizing I should also know what my actual fixed costs are, and then realizing I don't know how much my electric bill is on average because I just pay it when it shows up, and suddenly the forty-minute task is a whole life-audit and that's why I'm watching a sandwich video.

That's the pattern, I think. The tiny task is a doorway and behind the doorway is a much bigger room I don't want to be in. And I know the door, that's the thing. I know exactly how big the room is. So every time I get near the door I find a reason to go somewhere else.

The Mom thing made it worse, obviously. Now the task is also charged. Now opening the app is also implicitly answering her question, the real question, the are-you-okay one. And I don't want the answer the app gives to be the answer I report back. Because what if it's bad. Then I either lie to her next Tuesday or I tell her the truth and she gets that voice. The voice that means she's going to talk to Dad about it. The voice that means a follow-up email with a link to an article.

There are other things on the list too, since we're here. The dentist. I haven't been since college. There's a thing my dentist back home said about a tooth on the bottom right that I was supposed to keep an eye on and I have not kept an eye on it, I have not even looked at it in the mirror, I just chew on the other side now mostly. The friend trip. Nat sent a thing in the group chat in like, October, about maybe doing a weekend somewhere in spring, and I said "yes omg let's" and have not followed up once. The renter's insurance I keep meaning to set up. The fact that my emergency contact at work is still my college roommate from junior year and I haven't talked to her in a year.

It's all the same shape. The same flinch. The doorway to a bigger room.

I'm not going to do it tonight either. I can already tell. It's 10:40 and I have the energy of a wet towel. I'll do it this weekend. That's what I'm going to tell myself. This weekend. Saturday morning, with coffee. I'll write it on a sticky note. I won't actually write it on a sticky note. I'll think about writing it on a sticky note and that'll feel like enough until Saturday, when I'll wake up and want to walk with Avery instead.

Maybe I'll do the password reset tonight. Just that. Just one step. Reset the password and stop. That's a thing I could do in three minutes. Even that feels like a lot right now, which is so stupid I want to laugh.

Okay. Three minutes. After I close this.

We'll see.

entries.prompt: Last time something good happened — a bit of praise, a win at work, an unexpected nice moment — what did you do with it? Walk me through where you were when it landed and what you did with it in the minutes after.
entries.content:
Friday afternoon, like 4:30. Drew Slacked me. Just me, DM, not the channel. It said "hey — the doc you turned around on Wednesday was really clean. nice work." Lowercase. No exclamation point. But it said really. Really clean. Drew used the word really.

I read it three times. I read it like it was a contract. Really clean, nice work. Nothing in there to misread. There wasn't even room for the inner-tea-leaves thing because the message was too short and too direct. It just said the thing.

I sat at my desk and felt my whole face get hot. Like, embarrassingly hot. I was in the open part of the floor and I had to keep my face normal because if anyone looked over they'd see me grinning at my laptop like an idiot. I typed back "thank you!! glad it was useful" and then stared at the two exclamation points and didn't change them. Let them stand. Sent.

And then the minutes. Okay. Here's what I did with it.

First — and this is the part I want to write down because it's so dumb — I screenshotted it. I screenshotted my manager's two-line Slack message. I didn't send the screenshot anywhere. I just took it. Like I was preserving evidence. Like in three weeks when Drew is short with me about something else I can pull out the screenshot and go look, look, on this date Drew thought I was clean and good. I have it on my phone now. It's in my photos between a picture of the plant and a picture of a bagel.

Then I sat there for about four minutes not doing any work. Just kind of vibrating. I opened the doc Drew was talking about and reread it, trying to see what was clean about it. Trying to figure out what I'd done so I could do it again. Was it the structure? Was it the way I'd front-loaded the recommendation? Was it just that I'd turned it around fast? I was reverse-engineering the praise like an animal. Whatever the thing was, I needed to know, so I could put it in a little jar and use it later.

Then I texted Avery. I said "drew said my doc was clean :)" and Avery sent back "!!!!! see????" which was nice, that was nice, but I noticed a little flicker of — Avery doesn't get how big this is. Avery thinks this is a normal nice work thing. Avery doesn't know that this is the first time Drew has put a sentence like that in writing to me, in a DM, without anyone else around. Which is its own embarrassing inventory I'm apparently keeping.

Then — and this is the part I'm a little ashamed of — I walked to the kitchen for water I didn't need just to walk past Drew's desk. To see what Drew's face was doing. Like the praise wasn't real until I'd seen the face it came from. Drew was on a call, headphones on, looking at the screen, totally normal. Not thinking about me at all, obviously. The message had been a thing Drew did between two other things. I went back to my desk feeling slightly chastened, which is the right word. Like, okay, this was a normal kindness. Don't make it the whole sun.

But I couldn't stop making it the whole sun. For the next hour I worked harder than I'd worked all week. I cleaned up a different doc that didn't need cleaning. I answered Slacks I'd been letting sit. I felt — I felt big. Briefly. Like the proportions had inverted and I was the one taking up space instead of trying not to. It was such a strange feeling. I noticed it and then I noticed myself noticing it and then I tried not to scare it away by paying too much attention.

On the bus home I kept opening my phone, looking at the screenshot, putting the phone back. Opening it again. Like the message was going to disappear if I didn't check on it. There was this real fear, riding under the good feeling, that this was a fluke. That the next doc would be bad. That I'd peaked at clean and the only direction now was down. The good feeling was already, by 6pm, getting tangled up with the dread of having to repeat the good feeling. Which is — that's the trick, I think. The praise lands and immediately becomes a new floor I have to stay above. It doesn't sit on top of me as a gift. It slides under me as a standard.

I want to say I just enjoyed it. I want to say I came home and told Avery and we had wine and I let it be a nice Friday. Some of that happened. Avery did make pasta and I did tell the story, slightly embellished — I said Drew "really seemed happy" with the doc, which is more than what the message said, because in my head by then I'd inflated really clean into something more like a glowing review. I caught myself doing it and didn't correct it. Avery was happy for me. We ate. It was good.

But around nine I got quiet. Avery noticed. Avery said "you okay?" and I said "yeah just tired" which was true and also not the thing. The thing was I was already worrying about Monday. Already wondering what Drew would expect now. Already setting up the next audition.

Why can't I just hold it. Why does a nice thing become a debt. Drew said clean, I should be allowed to walk around for one weekend feeling clean, but I'm already trying to figure out how to be cleaner.

Okay. Here's what I'm going to try. I'm going to leave the screenshot in my photos and not look at it for the rest of the night. I'm going to let Friday be Friday. The doc was clean. Drew said so. That's a true sentence and it happened. I don't have to do anything with it. I don't have to earn it backward or forward. It just gets to be a thing that was true on a Friday afternoon in the open part of the floor, when my face went hot and I screenshotted my manager like a weirdo and then went and got water I didn't need.

The plant is fine. Bigger even. I keep forgetting that's a win too.

entries.prompt: Who's someone you haven't thought about in a while who came to mind recently — maybe a flash, maybe a longer drift? Where were you when they showed up, and what do you think pulled them back in?
entries.content:
Hannah. My college roommate junior year. The one who's still my emergency contact at work, which I noticed last week when I was thinking about all the things I haven't sorted out, and then she just kind of stayed in the corner of my head and showed up properly on Monday.

I was at the laundromat. The one two blocks down with the TV that's always on a channel nobody picked, like it just defaults to local news at 7pm. I had my clothes in the dryer and I was sitting on that bench they have, the wooden one with the carved initials, and I was scrolling, and I scrolled past someone's photo dump that had a girl in it who looked exactly like Hannah from the back. Just for a second. Same hair, same way of standing with the weight on one hip. Wasn't her. But my chest did the little thing where it skips a beat for a face it knows.

And then I was just — there. With Hannah. Not thinking about her exactly. More like remembering being around her. The way she'd leave her cereal bowl on the desk for two days and then suddenly clean the whole apartment in a frenzy. The way she laughed with her whole face. The fact that she made me try sushi for the first time and watched my face when I had wasabi by accident and laughed so hard she cried. The night before finals junior year when we both stayed up and didn't actually study, we just kept talking about our families until like 4am, and I told her stuff about my parents I hadn't told anyone, and she told me stuff about her brother, and it felt like the kind of conversation that would matter forever.

We haven't talked in over a year. Maybe a year and a half. I'm doing the math now and it might be closer to two. There wasn't a fight. There wasn't anything. We just stopped. She moved somewhere — I want to say Denver but I'm not actually sure, which is its own indictment — and I moved here, and the texts got further apart, and at some point one of us didn't reply and the other didn't follow up and that was that. I don't even remember which one of us dropped it last. I think it was me. I think she sent something like a "thinking of you, hope work is going okay" and I read it on the bus and meant to reply that night and didn't and then it had been three days and then a week and then it felt weird to reply and then it just — went away.

What pulled her back in. Partly the photo. Partly the emergency contact thing rattling around. But I think more than that — I've been thinking about how thin the scaffolding is, after the call with Mom, the bodega seltzer night, all of that. And Hannah is one of the beams that used to be there that isn't anymore. Not because she went anywhere bad. Just because I let go of the rope. I let go of a lot of ropes when I moved here. Not on purpose. Just by — not doing the small thing. Not replying. Not calling.

Sitting on the laundromat bench I tried to remember the last actual conversation we had. Not text. Phone, FaceTime, anything with a voice. And I couldn't. I genuinely could not pull one up. Which scared me a little. Because there was a year of my life where I talked to her every single day, multiple times a day, and now I can't remember the last time I heard her actual voice. That's a thing that happened and I didn't notice it happening.

The dryer beeped and I got up and folded my stuff badly, the way I always do, and I thought about texting her. Right then, in the laundromat. Just something. "Hey, you came to mind today, hope you're good." That's it. That's all it would take. And I didn't. I had the thought and then I had the next thought, which was — what if she doesn't reply. What if she replies politely but it's clear we don't have anything to say to each other anymore. What if the not-talking has calcified into actual distance and reaching across it just confirms it.

So I didn't text her. I walked home with the laundry bag over my shoulder feeling stupid about it. It's the same flinch as the savings app. The doorway thing. Open the door and the room is bigger than you can handle. Don't open it and the room can stay whatever you imagine it is. And what I imagine, I think, is that Hannah is somewhere being a person and would still be glad to hear from me. The text would probably be fine. It would probably be more than fine. She'd probably write back something warm and we'd do the catch-up dance and it would feel awkward for two messages and then good. That's probably what would happen.

I'm going to try to text her tomorrow. Not tonight, because tonight the fuck-it voice is already here and I don't want to send a real message in this mood. But tomorrow. Morning. Just — hey, you came to mind, hope you're well. Three sentences max. No big production.

I'm putting it here so I have to look at it.

The thing about Hannah, the thing I think actually pulled her back, is that she knew me before I started auditioning. That's the version of me she has. Sloppy, loud, would eat cereal for dinner, would cry about a movie, would sing in the shower badly. She doesn't know about Drew. She doesn't know about the screenshotting. She knew the person I was before all the scanning started, and some part of me wants to check in with her partly to check in with that person. To see if Hannah still recognizes me. To see if I do.

The plant looked good when I got home. I told it about Hannah, kind of. Not out loud. Just acknowledged it, the way you nod at something. Okay. Tomorrow. Three sentences.

entries.prompt: When did you last laugh — actually laugh, not the polite kind? Tell me where you were, what set it off, and who you were with.
entries.content:
Last night. Avery's apartment. We were trying to assemble this little side table Avery had ordered, one of those flat-pack things with the picture instructions and the bag of screws that comes with one extra screw nobody knows what to do with. We were on the floor in the living room with the pieces spread out and Avery had the instructions and I had the little allen wrench and we were going slow because we'd had wine with dinner and weren't fully tracking.

The thing was — at one point Avery held up two pieces that looked basically identical and said "okay so this one is A and this one is also A" and I looked at the instructions and they were both labeled A, like literally both pieces were stamped A on the side, and we sat there for a second trying to figure out which A was the real A, and Avery said, completely straight-faced, "one of them is lying." And I lost it. I don't know why that was the thing. It wasn't even that funny. But I started laughing and then I couldn't stop, and then Avery started laughing because I was laughing, and then we were both on the floor with these wood panels around us laughing about which A was the lying A, and I had that thing where you try to take a breath and instead you just laugh more, and my stomach actually hurt, and I had to put my forehead on the carpet for a minute.

Then Avery, still laughing, picked up one of the panels and held it up to the light like a detective and said "you. you've been deceiving us." And I went again. I was gone. I was wheezing. There was a moment where I tried to say something and just couldn't get the air. Avery looked at me trying to talk and that made Avery laugh harder, and then we were just looking at each other laughing about the fact that we were laughing, which is the best kind, the kind where the original joke is long gone and you're just riding the wave.

I don't remember the last time I laughed like that. I really don't. I laugh at work sometimes — the polite kind, yeah, but also genuine little laughs at something Reese says or a joke in a meeting. I laugh with Avery a lot, the easy laugh, the dog-was-the-most-alive-anyone-has-been kind. But the kind where your body takes over and you can't stop and your face hurts after — that's rarer than I'd realized until last night made me notice. I think the last one before this might have been with Hannah, actually. Which is wild. That's a long time.

What was different. I think it was that we were already a little unfocused from the wine, which lowers the bar, but also — there was no audience. There was nobody to perform for and nothing to get right. The table was going to get built or it wasn't. The instructions were dumb. We were on the floor. There was no version of me being graded. And Avery wasn't even being especially funny — "one of them is lying" is not a great line, objectively. It was just the timing, and the wine, and the fact that we'd been working on this thing for forty minutes and were both slightly punchy. The laugh was already loaded. Avery just pulled the trigger.

After we calmed down — and it took a while, every time we tried to get back to the table one of us would snort and we'd start again — we did finish it. Ish. There's one panel that I'm pretty sure is on backwards and we just decided we don't care. The table works. Things can be set on it. Avery declared it "structurally optimistic" which set me off again but only briefly.

I slept over and in the morning I saw the table from the bed and laughed a little, alone, just at the sight of it. The slightly-wrong panel. The evidence of us being idiots together. That counts as still laughing, I think. The aftershock of it.

The thing I want to write down is — I noticed, this morning on the bus to my place, that I felt different. Looser in my shoulders. Like the laugh had reset something. I don't know how long it'll last. Probably until I open Slack. But for one bus ride I felt like a person who had laughed until their stomach hurt the night before, which is maybe the most uncomplicated identity I've had in months. Person who laughed about a lying A. Person whose girlfriend says "structurally optimistic." Person on a bus.

I didn't text Hannah yet, by the way. I've been carrying that around. But I thought about her on the bus this morning and thought — she'd think the lying-A thing was funny. She would have laughed. She always laughed at the dumb stuff. Maybe that's the in. Maybe I just send her the photo of the slightly-wrong table and say "hannah this is my life now." No catch-up dance, no "hope you're well." Just a stupid table and a person who used to know me.

I'll think about it. The plant, by the way, has a new leaf. Tiny. Pale green. I noticed it this morning. Two wins. The leaf and the laugh.

entries.prompt: Is there something a friend or coworker said recently — even something small, in passing — that you've turned over more than once since? Tell me what they said, where you were, and what keeps pulling you back to it.
entries.content:
Reese said something on Wednesday that I keep coming back to and I can't tell if it's because it was nice or because it was the other thing.

We were waiting for the elevator, just the two of us, and Reese said — totally offhand, looking at the floor numbers — "you're a lot less stressed than I was at your point. it's kind of impressive."

That was it. The doors opened, we got in, somebody else got in on the next floor, the moment was over. Reese got off two floors before me and said "see ya" and I said "see ya" and then I was alone in the elevator for like four seconds with my own face in the mirror panel looking back at me.

Less stressed. Kind of impressive.

I have turned this over so many times. On the walk home Wednesday. In bed Wednesday night. On the bus Thursday morning. In the bathroom at work Thursday afternoon when I went in there for no reason. Right now.

First pass: nice. Reese was being nice. Reese is nice. Reese said a generous thing about me in the elevator and I should take it like a normal person and put it in the same drawer as Drew's "really clean" and move on.

Second pass: wait. Less stressed than Reese was? I am — I am not less stressed. I am extremely stressed. I am the person who screenshots Slack messages and reads tone into lowercase letters. If Reese thinks I'm less stressed than Reese was, then either Reese was somehow even worse than me, which I cannot picture because Reese walks around like someone who has never once worried about whether a meeting went well, OR — and this is the part that's been chewing on me — I am doing a really good job of hiding it. Which means the calm Reese sees is a performance. Which means the performance is working. Which should be a good thing. And it is, kind of. But it also means the version of me that gets praised at work is the version that's pretending, and the actual me, the one with the heart loud in the meeting, is invisible. Which I knew. But it's different to have someone confirm it from the outside.

Third pass: kind of impressive. Why did Reese say kind of. Why not just impressive. Kind of is a hedge. Kind of is what you say when you're being nice but you're also leaving yourself an out. Kind of impressive could mean impressive-for-someone-at-your-level, which is patronizing, or it could mean impressive-but-I-have-reservations, or it could just be how Reese talks. Reese hedges a lot. Reese says "kind of" and "sort of" all the time. I've heard Reese describe a sandwich as "kind of good." So it's probably nothing. It's probably just Reese's mouth.

But.

Fourth pass, and this is the one that keeps grabbing me: Reese was comparing. Reese said "than I was at your point," which means at some point Reese was at my point. Which means Reese has a model of where I am. Which means Reese is also doing the comparison thing, just from the other side. Reese is looking at me and measuring me against a previous version of Reese. And I have been so busy looking at Reese as the person ahead of me, the effortlessly competent one, that it never crossed my mind that Reese might be looking back at me at all. Like — Reese has thoughts about me. Reese has, apparently, been quietly noting how I'm doing. And the note Reese has is that I seem okay. Better than okay. Less stressed than Reese was. Kind of impressive.

And I have spent six months half-flinching away from Reese because I was scared of what the comparison would say about me, and the comparison this whole time has been — fine. Possibly even flattering. While I was busy assuming Reese saw me as a flailing junior trying to keep up, Reese was apparently seeing me as someone who's got it together. Which is so far off from how I feel that I don't know what to do with it.

The thing I keep getting stuck on, really, is — what's true. Am I actually less stressed than I think I am, and the inside experience is just dramatic? Or am I exactly as stressed as I think I am, and I'm just good at the costume? Because those are really different. The first one means I'm okay and don't know it. The second one means I'm not okay and nobody knows it. And I don't know which one I'd rather be.

I want it to be the first one. I want Reese to be right and my insides to be wrong. I want the calm to be real and not just well-painted.

I almost said something about it to Avery last night. I had the sentence loaded — "Reese said something kind of nice and kind of weird in the elevator." But I didn't, because I didn't want to spend twenty minutes unpacking it, and I knew if I started I would. Avery would say "that's a compliment, take the compliment" and I would nod and would still be turning it over on the bus the next morning. Which I am.

I think what I actually want is to ask Reese what Reese was like back then. What "your point" looked like from the inside. Because if Reese will tell me — even a little — that there was a stretch when Reese also felt like a fraud waiting to be caught, that would do something for me that no amount of Drew saying "really clean" can do. Drew is above. Reese is — recently where I am. Reese is the closer mirror.

Maybe that's the thing to take from it. Not the compliment exactly. The fact that Reese is a person I could actually talk to. The fact that the comparison goes both ways and isn't as scary from this side as I'd been assuming.

I don't know. I'll probably just keep turning it over for another three days and not do anything about it. But I noticed, writing this, that I'm smiling a little. Kind of impressive. I'll take it. Even with the kind of.

Plant has two new leaves now. I checked.

entries.prompt: Think of something you do almost every day without questioning it — a small routine, a habit, a way you spend a particular hour. Walk me through the last time you did it, and tell me what it's actually for.
entries.content:
The morning Slack check. Before I'm fully awake. Before coffee. Before I've put my feet on the floor sometimes. The phone is right there on the nightstand and I open it and I open Slack and I look.

This morning, like every morning. Alarm at 7:10. Hit snooze once, like always. At 7:19 I picked up the phone and the first app I opened was Slack. Eyes still half-closed. Just a quick scan of the unreads. Nothing urgent — a thread in the team channel from late last night where someone tagged a few people but not me, a Slackbot reminder, a message in the random channel about coffee in the kitchen on Friday. No DMs. Drew's status was gray. I checked Drew's status. I always check Drew's status. Why. It's 7:19 in the morning, Drew is asleep or making a kid's breakfast or whatever Drew does, the gray dot doesn't mean anything. But I check.

And then I lay there for another minute scrolling through the threads I didn't get tagged in, just to see what happened while I was asleep. Like I might have missed something. Like the night shift of my own job ran without me and I needed the briefing.

What is it actually for. That's the question and I've been chewing on it since I started writing this paragraph.

The honest answer is — it's a temperature check. I'm checking to see if anything bad happened overnight. If Drew sent a "can we chat in the morning" message. If something I sent on Tuesday is being criticized in a thread. If there's been a fire. The check is a way of finding out, before I'm fully conscious, whether today is going to be a normal day or a bad day. And the relief, when there's nothing — the small soft relief of "okay, no fires" — is the thing I'm actually after. I'm not checking Slack to learn things. I'm checking it to be allowed to get out of bed.

Which means the routine isn't a work thing. It's an anxiety thing wearing a work costume. I've turned the first conscious minute of my day into a scan of the one mirror I've been writing about for weeks. Drew's status. The team channel. Did I get tagged. Did anyone say my name. I am, before brushing my teeth, already auditioning. Already running the tape. The day hasn't started and I'm already in the meeting.

The thing that gets me is — nothing has ever actually been there. In two years of doing this, I cannot think of a single morning where the Slack check turned up something that genuinely needed my attention before 9am. Not one. The fire I'm checking for has never existed. I'm checking the smoke alarm in a house that isn't on fire and has never been on fire and probably won't be. And every morning the not-fire is a small relief that gets me out of bed, and every morning I have apparently decided that small relief is worth starting the day inside the work-shaped part of my head.

Avery doesn't do this. Avery checks the weather first. I watched once. Avery picked up the phone, opened the weather app, looked at it for two seconds, put the phone down, and got up. Like a person. Like someone who wanted to know if it was going to rain. I told Avery once that I check Slack first thing and Avery made a face — not a judgmental face, more like a "huh, that's a choice" face — and said "before coffee?" and I said "before everything" and Avery said "babe" in that voice that means Avery is not going to push but is also definitely noting it.

Other things I do every day without questioning. The walk to the coffee place even when I have coffee at home. The specific seat I take on the bus if it's available, the one near the back on the right. The way I open my laptop at work and the very first thing I do is, again, Slack. Then email. Then the doc I was working on yesterday. Same order every day. If the order gets interrupted — if someone catches me in the hallway before I've done the Slack-email-doc thing — I feel slightly off all morning, like I missed a step in a ritual.

I think the morning Slack thing is the foundational one though. The one that sets the temperature for the day. And the more I write about it the more I notice that the routine I do without questioning is the one that probably deserves the most questioning. It's not "I check work messages in the morning, like a responsible employee." It's "I have trained myself to wake up scared and look for the source of the fear before doing anything else." That's the actual shape of it. When I write it out like that it sounds insane. It also sounds completely normal, because I think a lot of people do this. Which doesn't make it less insane. It just means we're all doing it.

What would happen if I didn't. That's the experiment, I guess. Tomorrow morning I could pick up the phone and open the weather app first, like Avery. Or not pick up the phone at all. Just get up. See what happens. The Slack messages would be there at 9 when I sat down at my desk. They were going to be there anyway. The only thing the morning check buys me is forty extra minutes of pre-anxiety, which I have apparently been paying for daily, with the only currency I have, which is the first part of my day.

I'm not going to commit to anything. I know how that goes. I'll write "tomorrow I won't check" and then tomorrow at 7:19 my hand will go to the phone and I'll be in Slack before I've registered the decision, the same way I open new tabs to avoid the savings app, the same way I do all of it. The flinch is faster than I am.

But I noticed it. That's something. The plant has three new leaves now. I noticed that too. Different kind of noticing. Better kind, probably.

entries.prompt: Describe a time recently when you said something you didn't quite mean — too agreeable, too casual, too sharp, whatever direction it went. What had you wanted to say, and what came out instead?
entries.content:
Sunday. On the phone with Dad, of all people, which is rare because usually it's Mom and Dad is just the voice in the background saying "tell Kai I said hi." But Mom was at her sister's and Dad called instead, which I didn't even know he did, and we had this stilted little ten-minute conversation where neither of us really knew the rhythm.

He asked how the job was going. And I said "yeah, good, busy, you know." Which is what I always say. And then there was a pause and he said, "you happy there?"

Just that. Four words. In Dad's flat voice that doesn't telegraph anything.

And I said — fast, way too fast — "yeah, totally, it's been great." Totally. Great. I used both of those words. In the same sentence. About a job where I screenshot my manager's compliments and check the away light fifteen times a day and have a whole inner economy organized around whether Drew thinks I'm sharp.

What I had wanted to say. I don't even know if I knew what I wanted to say until after I'd said the wrong thing. But sitting there afterward, with Dad already moving on to something about the car needing new tires, I could feel the shape of the actual answer hanging in the air I hadn't used. Something like — I don't know yet. Something like — I think so, on the good days, but I can't really tell because I'm so inside it. Something like — ask me in a year. Something honest, even. I could've said "it's hard sometimes" and that would have been true and not even dramatic. I could have said "I don't know if I'm good at it yet."

Instead I said totally great. Like a brochure. Like a person reading off a card.

The thing is, Dad would have been fine with the real answer. Dad isn't Mom. Dad doesn't follow up. Dad would have said "yeah, takes a while" or "mm" and that would have been the end of it, and we'd have moved to the tires anyway. The actual cost of being honest with Dad would have been approximately zero. He wasn't fishing. He wasn't building a case. He was just asking, the way he asks, in his Dad way, where the question is the whole interaction and the answer isn't really the point.

But the totally great just came out. Reflex. Like somebody had pressed a button on me. I think I was scared that if I gave a complicated answer he'd worry, or worse, he'd tell Mom, and then Mom would call me on Tuesday with a real follow-up, the kind with prepared questions. So I gave him the smooth surface. The version where everything is fine. The version that closes the conversation cleanly.

And the second after I said it I felt this tiny crumple, like — oh. I just lied to my dad. Not a big lie. A polite lie. The kind everyone does on the phone. But still a lie, and to him, who was just trying to ask the one question he had.

What's weird is that I do this with Avery sometimes too. Avery asks "how was your day" and I say "fine" before I've even checked. The word is out before the inventory is done. And then I have to either correct myself, which is awkward — "actually, it was kind of bad" five seconds later — or just live inside the fine I declared. Usually I live inside it. And then later in the evening some weird mood comes out and Avery has to do detective work on a day I already labeled fine.

The pattern is so dumb when I see it. The honest answer is almost always available and almost always safe. The people asking aren't asking because they want to grade me. Dad isn't grading me. Avery isn't grading me. Even Mom, mostly, isn't grading me — she's just anxious in her own way. But I treat every "how are you" like a tiny version of Drew calling on me in the meeting. Quick, give a clean answer, don't take up space, don't be too much.

I think what I wanted to say to Dad, underneath all of it, was — I miss you. Which is wild. We weren't even talking about anything emotional. He asked about the job. But the wanting-to-say was actually about him. About the fact that he doesn't usually call, and he had called, and we had ten minutes, and I wasted them on totally great when what was actually going on inside me was — hi Dad. I haven't heard your voice in a while. The car tires are interesting. Tell me more about the car tires. Are you okay. Are you eating. Is your back still bothering you. I never ask him any of that. I let him ask me things and I bat them away with adjectives and then we hang up and I feel weirdly empty for the rest of the afternoon and don't know why.

I knew why this time. Because I had a chance to say a real sentence to my dad and I said totally great instead.

After we hung up I sat on the couch for a while. Avery wasn't home. The plant was doing its thing on the windowsill, four new leaves now, the smug little bastard. I thought about calling Dad back. Like, calling him right back and saying "hey, sorry, the answer to your question is more like — I don't know, but I think mostly yes." That would have been such a strange thing to do. He would have been confused. He would have said "okay, sweetie" in his confused voice. And I didn't do it. Of course I didn't do it. The window for the honest answer had closed and I'd let it close and that was the end of it for now.

Maybe I'll mention it next time. Maybe Tuesday when Mom calls I'll say something like "tell Dad the job is — actually kind of complicated, but I'm okay." Route it back through her. That sounds like something I would do. Hand the honest answer over secondhand because I couldn't get it out of my mouth in real time.

I don't know. The phone is on the coffee table. He's probably watching golf. I could call him. I'm not going to. But I could.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel jealous of someone — even a flicker, even someone you like? Tell me where you were when it hit, what set it off, and what you did with it after.
entries.content:
Tuesday, mid-afternoon, the all-hands. Reese presented. I knew Reese was presenting, it was on the calendar, I'd seen the slot. But I hadn't really braced for it because I figured it would be like every other update — fifteen minutes, here's what we did, here's what's next, polite questions, done.

It wasn't like that. Reese was good. Like, actually good. Not in a flashy way, in a — Reese had built a real narrative. Started with the problem, walked through the thing, ended with a clear ask. Made a joke in the middle that landed without trying. Took a question from someone on another team and answered it cleanly without saying um once. I was watching from my desk on the video stream and I could feel my stomach doing something. Like a slow tilt.

Then at the end Drew unmuted — Drew never unmutes in all-hands, Drew is a typer-in-the-chat person — and said "really nice work on this, Reese, this is exactly the kind of framing we need more of." Out loud. To everyone. Forty-something people on the call.

And I felt it. Right in the chest. Not a metaphor. A specific physical thing, like someone had pressed a thumb in just under my collarbone.

Jealous. That's the word. Even saying it in my head feels gross because I like Reese. Reese is the one who said the thing in the elevator. Reese is the one I've been telling myself is a possible friend, a closer mirror, all that. And the second Drew said "really nice work on this, Reese" out loud, in front of everyone, what I felt was — that should have been mine. Or — when does mine come. Or just — oh. Drew has the out-loud version of the compliment in there. Drew can do that. Drew chose to do it for Reese and not me.

The doc Drew called clean was a DM. A two-line lowercase DM with no exclamation point that I screenshotted like it was the Magna Carta. Reese got the whole-room version. The version where other people heard it. The version that becomes part of how Reese is talked about when Reese isn't in the room — which is the exact thing I wrote, weeks ago, that I wanted. I wrote it down. I named the want. And then I watched somebody else get it and felt the floor tilt.

I muted my video for a second, which I didn't need to do because it was already off, but I did the equivalent — I leaned back so my face wasn't in the frame even though no one was looking. Then I wrote in the chat "great job!!" with two exclamation points to Reese in a DM. Sent it before I could think about it. Then sat there feeling like I'd cheated somehow. Like I'd performed the supportive coworker thing while underneath I was — what. Counting. Adding up the column. Reese got the out-loud one. I got the lowercase one. Reese is ahead.

After the call I got up and went to the kitchen for water, the same water-trip I do whenever I need to walk somewhere. And on the way I had this thought that was so ugly I want to write it down so I have to look at it: I thought, what does Reese have that I don't. And I started cataloging. Reese has six more months. Reese has — I don't know — some natural thing with how to structure a presentation. Reese can take a question without freezing. Reese laughs at meetings without it sounding tactical. Reese ate a gas station hot dog at lunch and made it a story. The list got long fast. By the time I got to the kitchen I had built a whole case for why Reese was further ahead than I'd realized, and the case was making me feel worse, and I was the one building it.

I drank the water. I went back to my desk. I did not get any work done for about forty minutes. I just sat there clicking around tabs.

The thing that's bothering me, the part underneath, is — Reese has been kind to me. Reese said the thing in the elevator. Reese sent me a Slack last week with a link and "thought you'd like this." Reese is, by every available metric, on my side. And the first thing I felt when something good happened for Reese was a small private sourness. Not happiness for Reese. Not a clean "good for them." A flicker of — wait, where's mine. Like Drew's praise is a finite resource and Reese just took some of my share.

Which is the thing I do. I know it's the thing I do. The whole work part of my head is set up like there's one pie and everyone's slicing off it and if Reese gets a bigger slice then mine gets smaller. Drew probably has plenty of "really nice work" to go around. Drew said it once to Reese in an all-hands. Drew said it once to me in a DM. Both of those happened. Neither of them ate the other. But my chest did the thumb thing anyway.

I told Avery about it on the phone walking home. I said "Reese killed it in all-hands today and I felt weird about it." Avery said "weird how." I said "jealous, I think." And Avery, to Avery's credit, didn't say "oh you don't need to be." Avery said "yeah, that makes sense, you're around them all the time." Which was — that helped, actually. Avery didn't try to talk me out of it. Avery just let it be a normal thing to feel.

What did I do with it after. Honestly? Not much. I ordered Thai. The fuck-it voice came in. I watched something. The flicker mostly went away by the time I was eating, and what was left wasn't jealousy exactly, it was more like — okay, the room is bigger than just me and Drew. Reese is in it too. Other people are in it. People are getting things and not getting things and it isn't all about me. Which I knew. But seeing it with the thumb still under my collarbone hit different.

I'll see Reese tomorrow. I'll say something nice about the presentation in person. I'll mean it, mostly. The flicker will be filed away with the other flickers. The plant has five leaves now. Reese has nothing to do with the plant.

entries.prompt: There's a kind of compliment that lands differently than you expect — one you replay, one that itches, one that doesn't quite fit. Tell me about a time recently when someone said something good about you and it didn't sit the way you thought it would.
entries.content:
Friday after work. Avery and I were on the couch and Avery said, kind of out of nowhere, "you know what I love about you? you're so thoughtful with people." We'd been talking about something else — I think I'd just told a story about Priya, like I'd remembered it was Priya's birthday next week and was going to send her something — and Avery said it just like that, no buildup. You're so thoughtful with people.

I said "aw, thanks" and kissed Avery on the shoulder and we kept watching the show. That's what happened on the outside.

On the inside — the compliment did this weird thing where it kind of slid off. Like it didn't catch on anything. I noticed it sliding and tried to grab it and it was already gone. Avery said the thing and a part of me went, immediately, automatically — am I though. Like a reflex. The compliment hadn't even finished landing and I was already auditing it.

Because here's the thing. I had remembered Priya's birthday, yes. But I'd remembered it because the calendar app had pinged me. I hadn't done anything yet. I'd just said, out loud, that I was going to. And Avery heard "I'm going to send Priya something" and turned it into "you're so thoughtful with people" and I sat there knowing the gap between those two sentences. The intention isn't the thing. The thing is the thing. I haven't sent the thing.

And it pulled up this whole list, instantly, of all the ways I am not actually thoughtful with people. I haven't texted Hannah. I keep meaning to text Hannah and I don't. The group chat with Nat and Ben and Priya — Nat sent something three days ago about a podcast and I haven't responded, I just left it sitting. I didn't call Dad back after the totally-great call. I haven't asked Avery about the work thing Avery mentioned last weekend, the annoying coworker thing, I just nodded and moved on. If you opened up my actual behavior and looked at it, "thoughtful with people" is not the label that would come up. The label that would come up is more like "means well, doesn't follow through."

So Avery's compliment — sweet, generous, said with the kind of unguarded affection that's the whole reason I'm with Avery — landed on a person who isn't quite the person Avery was complimenting. And the gap made the compliment itch instead of settle.

The other thing, and this is the part I want to be careful about because it's tangled — Avery says nice things about me a lot. And I usually take them. The "really clean" from Drew I screenshotted. The elevator thing from Reese I've turned over a hundred times. But Avery's nice things sometimes just — pass through. Like the value of the compliment is somehow lower because Avery is contractually obligated to think well of me. Which isn't fair to Avery at all. Avery isn't contractually obligated to anything. Avery says the thing because Avery believes the thing. But there's a part of me that discounts compliments from inside the perimeter and only really banks the ones from outside it. Drew counts. Reese counts. Avery, who knows me best, counts least, which is so backwards it's embarrassing to write.

Maybe that's why it itched. Avery said the truest version of a compliment — generous, unprompted, based on years of watching me — and I treated it like junk mail. Sorted it without opening it. "You're so thoughtful with people." Yeah, sure, thanks. Meanwhile if Drew had said in a DM "you seem thoughtful with your team" I'd have framed it.

I keep wanting to fix this in real time and I can't. The fix would be — believe Avery. Take it. Let it land. But "let it land" isn't a thing you can decide to do. The compliment either lands or it doesn't and mine apparently has filters. The filter on Avery's compliment Friday was something like — Avery doesn't see the inside of my phone. Avery doesn't see the unanswered group chat or the un-texted Hannah. Avery sees the version of me that says "oh shoot, Priya's birthday" and counts the saying as the doing.

But also — wait. Avery does see those things. Avery has watched me be flaky. Avery has been on the receiving end of me being half-checked-out. Avery knows. And Avery still said it. Which means Avery either has lower standards than I do for what counts as thoughtful, or Avery is using a different definition. Maybe Avery's definition isn't about completion rate. Maybe it's about — I don't know — that I notice. That I remember Priya's birthday at all. That I think about Hannah even if I don't text her. That when Avery mentioned the coworker thing I didn't follow up but I did remember it, and I asked about it three days later when it came up again, and Avery noticed the asking. Maybe thoughtful, in Avery's accounting, is the noticing and not the doing-something-with-it.

That's a more generous accounting than mine. Mine is binary. Did I send the thing. Did I make the call. Avery's might be — does this person hold me in their head when I'm not in the room. And the answer there is yes. Avery is in my head all the time. So is Hannah, lately. So is Priya, this week. So is Dad.

Maybe the compliment didn't fit because I was hearing it as a verdict on my behavior and Avery was offering it as a description of my attention. Those are different. The behavior is patchy. The attention is — actually, it's there. It's pretty constantly there. I'm always thinking about people. I'm just not always doing anything with the thinking.

Which doesn't get me off the hook for the texts. I should still text Hannah. I should still send Priya something. I should still call Dad. The doing matters too. But maybe Avery wasn't wrong. Maybe Avery was seeing something true that I was filing as a lie because I was grading myself on the wrong axis.

I don't know. The compliment is still sitting there in my head two days later, which is its own answer. The ones that fit don't sit. They just dissolve into you. The ones that don't fit are the ones you keep turning. Avery said something true and I'm going to be turning it for a while.

Plant has six leaves. I noticed for Avery, kind of. Wanted to point it out. Didn't. Will tomorrow.

entries.prompt: How do you want this year to be different from last year? Pick one specific thing — not a resolution, just something you've been quietly hoping shifts — and tell me what it would actually look like if it did.
entries.content:
The thing I've been quietly hoping shifts is — I want fewer mornings that start with the phone. That's the specific one. Not a big abstract "be less anxious" thing. Just: the first ten minutes. The 7:19 to 7:29. I want those back.

I keep thinking about it because I wrote that whole entry weeks ago about the morning Slack check and I named it, I called it what it is, I said out loud in my own notebook that I have trained myself to wake up scared and look for the source of the fear before doing anything else, and then the next morning at 7:19 my hand went to the phone and opened Slack before my eyes were fully open. So naming it didn't do anything. The flinch is faster than the noticing. I knew that going in. But I still kind of hoped naming it would crack it a little, and it didn't.
