What would it actually look like if it shifted. Let me try to be honest about this instead of writing the brochure version.

The brochure version is: I wake up, I stretch, I drink water, I journal for ten minutes, I make coffee with intention, I look out the window, I arrive at work centered. That's not real. That's a person from an Instagram carousel. I would last two days.

The real version is smaller. The real version is: I wake up and the first thing I look at isn't Slack. That's it. That's the whole shift. It could be the weather, like Avery does. It could be the ceiling. It could be the plant — it's right there on the windowsill, six leaves now, I could literally just look at it. The point isn't to start a routine. The point is to take the first ten minutes of the day out of Drew's hands. Out of the audition. Just those ten minutes.

Because here's what I notice — when the first thing I do is check Slack, the whole day is already shaped by 7:20. My shoulders are already up. My head is already in the meeting. By the time I'm actually in the office I've been working, in some emotional sense, for two hours, and most of that work was just bracing. Whereas on the rare mornings — and they exist, I can think of two in the last month — when I forgot the phone, or it was dead, or I got up because Avery was up and made coffee first, those mornings I arrived at the office a different person. Not relaxed, exactly. Just — not pre-stressed. The day got to start where the day actually starts, which is when I open my laptop at 9.

If this shifted — really shifted, not for one heroic week but as a real thing — I think what would change is small but everywhere. I'd get to have a body in the morning instead of just a brain. I might notice if I was hungry. I might notice if I'd actually slept. I might walk to the coffee place and have it be a walk, not a buffer between two periods of phone-staring. The barista would say something and I'd actually be there to hear it.

And further out — I think the morning sets the temperature for what kind of person I am at work that day. The mornings I don't pre-check, I'm a little more — I want to say generous? With myself, with other people. A meeting goes weird and I don't immediately go into the spiral, because the spiral isn't already spinning. Drew's status is gray and I notice I haven't checked it, and the not-checking is its own little surprise. Reese says something and I hear it as something Reese said, not as data to be cataloged.

I'm not sure I can hold all of that. But the morning thing — that one I might actually be able to do. And it might do more than its size.

What I'm scared of, writing this, is making it into another thing on the list. Another doorway with a bigger room behind it. The savings app. The Hannah text. The dentist. The not-checking-Slack. Same shape. I name them and then I don't do them and then I write about not doing them and feel like the writing was the doing.

So let me try not to do that here. Let me make this small. Tomorrow morning, when the alarm goes off at 7:10, I'm going to put the phone face-down before I hit snooze. That's the whole thing. Not a vow. Not a system. Just one motion. Phone face-down. If I pick it up anyway at 7:19 and open Slack, I open Slack. But the face-down adds a half-second of friction. A half-second where my hand has to flip the phone over before it can get to the app. Half a second is more than I have right now. Right now there's no friction at all, the flinch and the open are the same gesture.

Half a second isn't going to fix anything. But it's the only kind of thing I actually believe I can do, and the only kind of thing I haven't already broken by promising too big.

What I want, underneath all of this — and I think this is the real answer to the prompt — is to spend less of this year scared before I have a reason to be. Last year I was scared all the time and the reasons came and went, mostly went, mostly never showed up. The fear was its own weather. I don't want fear to be the weather this year. I want it to be a thing that arrives when something actually happens, and leaves when the thing is done. That's a big shift. I can't engineer it. But the morning is where the weather gets set. Maybe if I can keep the first ten minutes from being scared, the rest of the day has a different chance.

Phone face-down. Plant on the sill. We'll see what 7:19 looks like.

entries.prompt: What's something you're better at than you let yourself believe? Pick a specific moment recently when the evidence was right in front of you — and tell me what you did with it.
entries.content:
Wednesday. The thing with the cross-team meeting that I'd been low-key dreading for like a week. Two people from the other team I've never really talked to, plus Drew, plus me, and the agenda was basically — figure out how the timeline works given that their thing depends on our thing depends on their other thing. I'd put it on the calendar with a little dread tag in my head. I'd been carrying it around since Monday like a small rock.

What actually happened was that we got in there and the two people from the other team started talking and within about four minutes it was clear they didn't agree with each other. Like, at all. One of them — I think his name is Marcus — kept saying his team needed our deliverable by the end of the month and the other one — Priya, different Priya — kept saying that wasn't possible because of something on their side, and Drew was kind of letting them go back and forth, and I was sitting there with my notebook open watching it.

And at some point — I don't even remember deciding to — I said, "okay wait, can I just try to map this out? because I think you're talking about two different things." And then I did. I went up to the whiteboard. I drew a little diagram. I labeled the two timelines. I pointed at where they crossed and said, "this is where the dependency is, right? and Marcus, you're saying you need the output of this part by the 28th, and Priya, you're saying you can't start this part until the 22nd, which means there's only six days here, which is what's actually the problem." And both of them stopped and looked at the board and Marcus said "yeah, that's the problem" and Priya said "right, yes" and then we spent the rest of the meeting talking about the actual six-day thing instead of talking past each other.

Drew didn't say anything during the diagram part. Drew just watched. At the end Drew said "good, this was helpful" in that flat Drew way and we all left.

I want to be careful here because I know what I usually do, which is take a moment like that and immediately find the angle where it wasn't actually that good. The diagram was crooked. I said "um" twice. Marcus probably could've gotten there himself in another minute. Drew said "good" and not "great." I have all the deflations queued up.

But here's the thing. I did the thing. I saw two people not talking to each other and I figured out why and I drew it on a whiteboard and the meeting got unstuck. That's a real sentence. That happened. And the version of me I usually carry around in my head — the one who freezes when called on, who gives twelve-word answers, who rehearses on the bus — that version could not have done that. That version would have sat there taking notes, hoping nobody asked. The version that did do that was someone else. Or — okay, the version that did that was also me. Which is the part that's hard to write.

Because the evidence was right in front of me. I am, apparently, sometimes, a person who can stand up in a meeting with people I don't know and Drew watching and just — solve a small thing. I had been thinking about the timeline for a week, like with the meeting weeks ago, but this time the thinking actually came out. I was the one with the clearest picture of what was happening and I let myself be the one with the clearest picture, out loud, with a marker in my hand.

What did I do with it. That's the prompt. And the honest answer is — I deflated it on the walk back to my desk. By the time I sat down I was already telling myself it wasn't that big a deal, anyone could have seen the thing, it was just a diagram. Marcus probably thought I was being condescending. The marker squeaked. Etc. By 5pm it was filed away as a basically-okay meeting and I was back to scanning Drew's status.

That's what I do with it. I let it dissolve. The bad moments — the two-second pause, the lame answer, the lowercase "got a sec" — those I keep. I screenshot those. I turn those over for weeks. The good moments I let go of inside an hour. The ledger is rigged. One side gets compound interest, the other side gets spent on the walk to the kitchen.

What I think I'm better at than I let myself believe is — seeing the structure of a thing. Not the verbal-quick-answer thing, which is what I keep grading myself on. I am not the quickest mouth in the room. I don't have the one-liner. I freeze when called on, sometimes, still. But if you give me a week to sit with something, or even an hour in a meeting where I can listen, I can see how the pieces connect. That's what the doc Drew called clean was. That's what the diagram was. That's actually what I've been doing on this project for months, quietly, in the background, without giving myself credit for it because it doesn't look like the thing I think competence looks like. Competence in my head looks like Reese in the all-hands. Smooth, fast, funny. I'm not that. But I'm something else and the something else is real and other people apparently see it.

That's what I want to write down before the deflation does its work. I am better at seeing the shape of things than I let myself believe. Drew has now said it twice — once in the DM, once with "good, this was helpful," which isn't out-loud praise but isn't nothing. Marcus and Priya looked at the board and got unstuck. I did that. Not by being the smoothest person in the room. By being the one who'd been quietly thinking.

I'm not going to screenshot anything. I'm going to leave this here in the notebook instead. The diagram was crooked and the meeting got unstuck. Both true. The plant has seven leaves now. I keep forgetting to count and being surprised.

entries.prompt: When was the last time you felt genuinely surprised by someone — they did or said something you wouldn't have predicted? Tell me what they did, where you were, and what it shifted in how you see them.
entries.content:
Saturday. We were at the grocery store, the big one over by the park, the one that's always too cold. Avery and I were doing the weekly thing, splitting the list — Avery had produce and dairy, I had pasta and the random middle aisles. We were going to meet at the registers in fifteen.

I was in the cereal aisle when my phone buzzed. Drew. On a Saturday. Drew texting me. Drew has my number for emergencies, technically, but Drew has never used it. Not once. So when "Drew" came up on the screen my stomach did the elevator drop and I just stood there in front of the granolas trying to read the message through the panic.

It said: "hey — sorry to bug on a weekend. totally ignore till monday. but did you save the version of the doc from before the tuesday edits? can't find it on my end."

That was it. No urgency. The sorry-to-bug. The "totally ignore till monday." The lowercase. Drew was — apologizing. To me. For texting on a Saturday. About a doc.

I read it three times in the cereal aisle. The thing is — Drew, in my head, doesn't apologize. Drew is the one who decides if things are okay. Drew is the weather. The idea that Drew could be on the other end of a text feeling slightly awkward about bothering someone on a weekend — like a person, like a normal person who knows it's weird to text an employee on Saturday — had genuinely not occurred to me. I have spent so long building up Drew as this monolithic figure whose opinion sits like four hundred pounds in my chest that I forgot Drew is also a person who has, presumably, been on the other side of a Saturday text from their own boss at some point and remembered what it felt like.

I texted back "no worries! yeah I have it, I'll send it now" and went and stood in the pasta aisle and actually pulled the doc up on my phone and forwarded it from my work email to Drew. The whole thing took maybe four minutes. Drew replied "lifesaver, thank you. enjoy your weekend." Two sentences. No expectation of further reply.

I stood there with rigatoni in one hand and my phone in the other and I felt — weird. Not bad weird. Disoriented weird. Like a piece of furniture I'd been navigating around for a year had been moved six inches and the room was the same but also different.

Because the thing about Drew, in my head, is that Drew is always the one with the power. Drew chooses when to say nice work. Drew chooses when to send "got a sec." Drew chooses what to put in the chat in the all-hands. The whole one-way mirror thing. I look at Drew and Drew looks at the work, and I read Drew's face for clues about whether I'm doing okay. That's the model. And in that model Drew doesn't text on a Saturday saying sorry to bug. In that model Drew either texts urgently because something is wrong, or doesn't text at all. There's no middle. There's no "I lost the doc and feel bad asking."

But the middle is apparently where Drew lives. Drew lost a doc on a Saturday and felt bad asking and texted apologetically and thanked me and that was the whole thing. That was the actual interaction. Not a referendum on me. Not a test. A person needing a doc.

What it shifted, I think — and I'm still figuring this out, I literally just got home an hour ago — is the size of Drew. Drew has been enormous in my head. Drew has been the size of the whole job. And the text didn't make Drew small, exactly, it just made Drew — proportional. A person who manages me and also forgets where files are and also feels weird about texting on weekends. Someone with a Saturday. Someone whose Saturday I had briefly disrupted by being slow to respond because I was reading the message four times like it was a code.

I told Avery about it at the registers. Avery said "wait, your boss is being polite to you?" and laughed, and I laughed too, but I felt the laugh — Avery was right. That's what was surprising. Drew was being polite. Drew was treating me like someone whose Saturday matters. Which, when I write it down, is not actually a wild thing for a manager to do. That's how a normal manager treats a normal employee. The wild thing is that I'd somehow constructed a Drew in my head who wouldn't.

I keep thinking about all the Slacks I've decoded for tone. The lowercase G's. The away light. The "got a sec." I have built a whole theology around a person who, evidence increasingly suggests, is just a slightly tired manager doing their job and trying to be reasonable about it. The theology is mine, not Drew's. Drew didn't ask to be the weather. I made Drew the weather because I needed something to look at.

Walking home with the bags, Avery was telling me something about Avery's mom and I was half-listening and half just feeling this little internal rearrangement. I'm not going to suddenly stop checking Drew's status on Monday. I know I'll do the thing. But there's a tiny part of the picture that's different now. Drew apologized to me for texting on a Saturday. That's a real sentence and it happened. The person at the other end of the four hundred pounds is — just a person. With weekend plans. Who lost a doc.

Plant has eight leaves. I told Avery this time. Avery said "you've been counting?" and I said "kind of." Avery smiled in the way that means I just learned something tiny about you. That happened too, today. Two surprises. One from Drew, one I gave Avery without meaning to.

entries.prompt: Think of a place you go regularly — a coffee shop, a route you walk, a chair in your apartment, somewhere at work — and tell me what you're usually doing in your head while you're there. Pick the last time you were in that spot and walk me through it.
entries.content:
The chair in the corner of the bedroom. The one that's not really a chair, it's more of a — I don't know what to call it. A pile. It started as a chair, an actual chair I bought off Craigslist when I moved in, but it's been a clothes chair since approximately week three. There's a sweater on it that I haven't worn since November. There's a tote bag from a work thing. There's the jeans I wore Tuesday and Wednesday and Thursday and decided weren't quite dirty yet. The chair underneath all of that is, technically, blue.

I sit on the floor next to it. That's the part I want to get to. There's this little patch of floor between the chair and the window, maybe two feet wide, and I sit there sometimes. With my back against the radiator, which is usually warm, and my feet kind of tucked under me. It's not comfortable. It's not where you'd design a sitting spot. But I end up there a few times a week, mostly in the evening, mostly when I've come home and haven't decided yet what kind of night it's going to be.

Last night was one. I got home around 7, dropped my bag by the door, took my shoes off, and instead of going to the couch I went to the bedroom and sat on the floor next to the chair. Didn't turn the overhead light on. There was just the streetlight coming in through the blinds, the orange-y kind, and the radiator clicking. The plant was on the sill above me — eight leaves, getting close to nine I think, there's a little nub — and I could see it from below, this little silhouette against the window.

What was I doing in my head. Honestly — looping. I was looping about a thing Reese had said in standup that morning, something completely innocuous about a deadline, and I'd been carrying it around all day like a rock in my shoe trying to figure out if it was a dig. By the time I got home and sat down on that floor I was on like loop fourteen of "did Reese mean it that way" and I knew I was on loop fourteen and I couldn't stop. So I just sat there and let it run. Didn't try to fix it. Didn't open my phone. Just sat with my back against the radiator while my brain played the same five-second clip over and over.

That's what the floor spot is for, I think. I just realized this writing it. The couch is a doing place. On the couch I watch something or scroll or eat or talk to Avery. The bed is a sleeping place, mostly, or a phone-scrolling-pretending-to-sleep place. The kitchen table is a working place. But the floor by the chair is — it's the only spot in the apartment where I don't expect anything of myself. I'm not trying to relax there. I'm not trying to be productive. I'm just sitting on the floor like an animal. The chair is buried in clothes so I can't even sit in it properly, which somehow makes it the most honest piece of furniture I own. It's not pretending.

After a while — maybe twenty minutes, I don't actually know — the loop quieted down. Not because I solved it. I didn't solve it. Reese probably didn't mean anything and even if Reese did it's a thing I can ask about Monday or just let be. The loop just got tired. It ran out of new angles. And what was left underneath was — okay, I'm hungry. I haven't eaten. My back kind of hurts. The radiator is too hot now actually. The streetlight is making a stripe on the wall that's sort of pretty. Small body things. Small room things.

That's the thing about that spot. It's where I go when I need to wait something out. I don't think I'd ever articulated that before. The other places in the apartment are for doing — even resting, on the couch, is a kind of doing, you're consuming something. The floor by the chair is for waiting. For letting whatever's running just run until it stops.

I should say — I do also use it for less noble things. Sometimes I sit there and cry a little, briefly, about nothing I can name, and then get up and order Thai. Sometimes I sit there and stare at the chair pile and think I should do laundry and then don't. Sometimes I sit there and have whole imaginary conversations with Drew where I say the thing I should have said in the meeting, and the imaginary Drew nods slowly and says "huh, good catch," and I know it's pathetic and I do it anyway. The floor spot is not a holy place. It's a holding place.

What I noticed last night, though — and this is probably why I picked this for the prompt — is that I came out of it feeling a little better. Not fixed. Just — the rock in my shoe was gone. And I'd done nothing to remove it. I'd just sat there long enough for it to dissolve. Which is interesting because most of what I do with bad feelings is try to manage them, decode them, talk to Avery about them, write about them here. The floor spot is the one place I just — let them be. Wait them out. And it works, sometimes, in a way the managing doesn't.

I got up around 7:45, my legs full of pins and needles, and went to the kitchen and made eggs. Just eggs. Like a person who knows what to do at dinnertime. Texted Avery about something dumb. Watered the plant a little. Sat on the couch and did the normal evening things. The Reese loop was gone. I don't even know exactly when it left. Sometime on the floor. Between the radiator clicking and the streetlight stripe.

I'm not going to clean off the chair. I just decided. The pile is part of it. The chair being unusable is what makes the floor next to it a place. If I cleaned the chair I'd just sit in the chair and it wouldn't be the same. I'd be in a chair, looking at a phone, like every other spot. The pile is doing a job.

Plant might be at nine by tomorrow. The nub is real. I checked again before bed.

entries.prompt: Why do you think you've been carrying small dread around lately — the meeting on the calendar, the chair you don't sit on, the text you haven't sent? Pick one of them and tell me what you imagine happening if you actually walked toward it instead of around it.
entries.content:
The Hannah text. Let's just say it. It's been on the list since the laundromat — that's, what, six weeks now? Maybe more. I keep losing count. The doorway one. The one where the room behind it could be anything and so I keep walking past.

I almost did it tonight. I had my phone in my hand on the couch, I had her contact pulled up — which was its own little jolt, seeing her name there, the contact photo from college that I never updated, her with that ridiculous Halloween costume from senior year, a pirate I think, the eyepatch crooked. I scrolled up to the last text thread between us. November of last year. Her: "thinking of you, hope work is going okay 💙" Me: nothing. The blank space underneath where my reply was supposed to go. Just — white. The whole rest of the screen. I looked at it for a while.

What's the dread, exactly. Let me try to actually name it instead of just gesturing at it. It's not that she'll be mean. Hannah is incapable of being mean, she literally cannot do it, I've watched her try. It's not that she won't reply. She will. She'll reply within an hour, probably, with too many emojis, and she'll say "OH MY GOD HI" in all caps because that's how she texts. I know all of this. I know exactly how the first message back will look.

The dread is what comes after that. Message two. Message three. The catch-up dance. Where we both have to admit, in the gentle way you do, that we don't know each other's lives anymore. She'll ask about the job and I'll have to figure out how much to say. She'll mention something — a partner I didn't know she had, a move I didn't know about — and I'll have to do the thing where you act like you knew, or like you're not surprised, or like the not-knowing is fine, all of which will be lies. And then there'll be the moment, somewhere around message seven, where one of us has to decide whether to push toward an actual conversation — a phone call, a FaceTime — or let it die back down into another year of nothing. And I'm scared of both options. I'm scared she'll push and I'll have to be on a phone with her and find out we don't fit anymore. I'm scared she won't push and I'll have to feel us not fitting from a polite distance.

That's the dread. Not the text. The thing the text starts.

Okay. What if I actually walked toward it.

Let me try to picture it for real. I send the message tomorrow morning, on the bus, before I can talk myself out of it. Not the three-sentence catch-up version. The lying-A version. The one I thought of weeks ago. Just a photo of the slightly-wrong side table and "hannah this is my life now." That's it. No "hey, you've been on my mind." No "sorry it's been so long." No catch-up bid. Just a stupid picture and a stupid line, the kind of thing I would have texted her in college without thinking, the way she texted me a photo of her shoe one time with the caption "shoe."

She'll write back. I know she will. She'll say something like "WAIT IS THAT THE LYING A" — no, she won't get the joke, she won't have the context, but she'll say "this is incredible" or "the leg is BACKWARDS" or whatever Hannah thing. And then maybe she'll ask "how are you" and maybe I'll just send another picture, the plant, "she has nine leaves now," and she'll send something back, and we'll be doing it. Not catch-up. Just hanging out, basically. The way we used to. Sending each other dumb things.

That's the version I want to picture. Not the formal reentry. The side-door reentry. Where we skip the part where we have to acknowledge the gap and just — start sending pictures of stuff again, like nothing happened, like a year and a half is just a long pause in a conversation we never officially ended.

And maybe she'll want more than that. Maybe she'll write back and say "okay but actually hi, how are you, can we talk this weekend" and then I'll have to choose. And maybe I'll say yes and we'll get on the phone and it'll be awkward for ten minutes and then it'll be Hannah's voice in my ear, the actual voice I haven't heard in maybe two years, and she'll say something about her mom or her job or someone she's seeing and I'll laugh in the way I only laugh with her and we'll both notice the laugh and not say anything about it. That's the version I'm not letting myself want. That's the actual room behind the door. That's the room I've been pretending isn't there because if I let myself want it and don't get it I'll have lost something I've been carrying as merely-dormant this whole time.

But here's what I think I'm finally starting to understand, sitting on the floor by the chair the other night, the radiator clicking — the dormant version is also a loss. I've been protecting the friendship by not testing it, but the unpushed friendship is already a thinner thing than the pushed one, even if the push goes badly. Right now Hannah is a contact photo and a blank reply. That's already what she is. The text can't make her less than that. The text can only make her more.

The plant has nine leaves now. I checked this morning. The nub became a leaf. It just took a few weeks. The leaf wasn't dramatic about its arrival. It just showed up.

I'm going to send the picture tomorrow on the bus. The lying-A side table. "hannah this is my life now." I'm writing it here so I have to look at it. I've written that sentence in this notebook before, about her, and not done the thing. I know. But this time the dread is a little smaller because I named what was actually in it, which is not Hannah, which is me being scared of finding out. And that's a thing I can walk toward, badly, with a stupid photo, on a bus, before my hand has time to flinch.

If she doesn't reply, she doesn't reply. If she replies and it's flat, it's flat. Both of those are still more than blank.

Tomorrow. Bus. Photo. Send.

entries.prompt: Tell me about a time recently when you caught yourself doing the math on what someone else could handle hearing — softening something, leaving a piece out, picking the version that wouldn't cost them anything. What did you say, and what was the version you didn't say?
entries.content:
Tuesday call with Mom. The regular one, the Tuesday one, the one I already brace for. She asked how I was, in the way she asks, which is two questions stacked — the surface one and the actual one. Surface: how are you. Actual: are you eating, are you sleeping, is the relationship okay, is the job okay, is the money okay, are you okay.

I said "I'm good, busy week, but good." Five words doing the job of fifty.

Then she asked about Avery, and I said "Avery's good, we built a side table on Friday, it's slightly crooked but it works." She laughed. She liked that. She always likes the small concrete thing. And then she said "and the job? you sound a little tired" and I said — I could feel myself doing it — I said "yeah just been a lot going on, but it's good, I had a thing in a meeting last week that went well actually, I drew a diagram and it kind of unstuck the whole thing." I gave her the diagram story. The whiteboard. Marcus and Priya. Drew saying good, this was helpful.

She loved it. She said "oh sweetie, that's wonderful, see, you're doing great." And I said "yeah, it was nice." And we moved on to her sister and the new dog her sister got and whether the dog is okay around Mom's allergies.

Here's what I didn't say.

I didn't say that the diagram thing was three weeks ago and I've been deflating it ever since. I didn't say that the version of the meeting in my head has been shrinking — by the next morning the marker squeaked, by the end of the week Marcus probably could've gotten there himself, by yesterday it was barely a story at all, and I pulled it out for Mom because it was the one thing in recent memory I could put on the table that sounded like I was thriving. I didn't say I'd been mining the last month for anything that scanned as a win and that was the only piece of ore I could find.

I didn't say that on Monday I'd cried a little on the floor by the chair for no specific reason. I didn't say that Reese said something in standup and I spent the whole evening looping on whether it was a dig and eventually had to just sit on the floor until the loop got tired. I didn't say I check Drew's status before I check the weather. I didn't say I screenshot compliments. I didn't say I almost called Dad back two weeks ago to give him a different answer to the question he'd already asked, and didn't, and have been a little ashamed of it ever since. I didn't say that I haven't opened the savings app since she asked about it, that the whole thing is still sitting there in a tab somewhere, that her question is still unanswered in the only place it actually counts.

I didn't say I miss her sometimes in a way that surprises me. I didn't say it because if I said it she would hear it as a problem to solve, and within a day there'd be a flight booked, or a care package, or a follow-up with prepared questions. I love her for that. I also can't carry it. The thing she gives when she's worried is more than I can absorb, and so I edit upstream. I take the version of me that would cause her to worry and I sand it down before it hits the phone.

The math is always the same. What can Mom handle. Mom can handle the diagram story. Mom can handle Avery and the side table. Mom cannot handle "I'm a little lost." She can't, not because she's weak — she's not — but because for her "a little lost" is a five-alarm fire and I don't have the energy to talk her down from a fire I started by being honest. So I pick the version that costs her nothing. And it costs me — what. Some small amount of being known by my mother. Which I am apparently willing to spend, every Tuesday, in exchange for a shorter call.

The thing that bothers me is that I do this with everyone. The math is just loudest with Mom because the gap between the surface and the actual is widest. With Dad it's "totally great." With Avery it's "fine" before I've checked. With Drew it's exclamation points I add and then take off and then add again. With Hannah — well, with Hannah it's a year and a half of not even attempting the math because the calculation felt too expensive to start. I am running this calculation constantly and I never asked anyone if they wanted me to.

What's the version I didn't say to Mom. If I'm honest. It would have been something like — "I think I'm okay but I genuinely don't know, and the not-knowing is the loud part. I had a good moment in a meeting and I can't hold onto it. Avery is being lovely and I keep flinching. The savings thing you asked about — I haven't looked. I keep meaning to. I'm sorry." That's maybe forty seconds of talking. Mom would have done something with it I couldn't predict, which is the actual fear. Not that she'd react badly. That she'd react in a way I couldn't manage from a thousand miles away on a Tuesday at 7pm with the Thai food already on its way.

And the smallest version — the one I really didn't say, the one underneath all the others — is just: I wish you lived closer. I wish you could come over. I wish I could sit on your couch instead of mine for an hour. That one I can't say at all because if I said it she'd cry and then I'd cry and then we'd both be wrecked and the call would end with me promising to fly home for a weekend I can't actually afford. So it stays in the floor-spot category. Things I sit with, not things I send.

Plant has nine leaves. I told her about the plant. She liked that too. She said "see, you're keeping things alive." I said "yeah." That part was true.

entries.prompt: Last time you were really angry — even a low simmer counts — what set it off, and what did you do with it in the next hour?
entries.content:
Thursday. The 4:30 meeting that got rescheduled to 5:15 by Drew with a "sorry, has to slide, can you stay?" Slack at 4:22. I said "yep np" and went and refilled my water and sat back down.

Here's the thing I want to be honest about. The reschedule wasn't the thing. The reschedule was fine. I had nothing after work, Avery was at a thing, I would have been on the couch by 7 either way. The thing was that on the way back from the water I checked Reese's calendar — I do this, apparently, I check Reese's calendar to see what Reese is doing — and Reese had been in a 1:1 with Drew at 3:30 that I didn't know about. Just a half-hour block. "Drew / Reese." Recurring. I clicked into it. It's been recurring since November.

November. So for like four months Drew has been having a regular 1:1 with Reese that I am not having. I have my regular 1:1 with Drew, a half hour every other Tuesday, which I assumed was the standard thing. But Reese has weekly. Reese has double what I have. And I've been sitting at my desk for four months not knowing, screenshotting compliments and checking away lights, while Reese has been having a half-hour conversation with Drew every single week about — what. About whatever Reese wants to talk about. About Reese's career. About the kind of framing Drew wants more of.

I felt the heat in my face first. Then a kind of static in the ears. Then the simmer.

Not at Reese. I want to be clear about that, even though my first move was very much at Reese — I sat there for a minute composing imaginary things to say to Reese, like "must be nice to have weekly time with Drew" said with a smile, the kind of comment you can't take back. I didn't send anything. I just composed it in my head with a little rage. Then the simmer turned, like it always does, and landed where it was actually pointed, which is Drew. Why does Reese have weekly and I have biweekly. Did I not ask for it. Was I supposed to ask for it. Did Reese ask for it and I didn't know you could ask. Or did Drew offer it, and offer it to Reese and not me, which would mean Drew thinks Reese needs more attention or deserves more attention or is more worth investing in, which is the exact thing I have wanted Drew to think about me, which Drew apparently thinks about Reese instead.

The 5:15 meeting happened. I was barely there. I nodded. I said two things. I tracked Drew's face the whole time looking for evidence of — I don't even know what. Some signal that Drew thought less of me than of Reese. Drew's face was just Drew's face. Tired, a little distracted, said "thanks all" and dropped off.

I closed the laptop and went to the floor by the chair. Didn't even take my work clothes off. Just sat down with my back to the radiator.

The simmer kept going for a while. I let it. I did the imaginary conversation with Drew where I said "I noticed Reese has weekly and I have biweekly, can we talk about that," and the imaginary Drew said "oh, do you want weekly? I just figured you didn't." Which, in the imagined version, made me feel both vindicated and stupid, because of course the answer might just be that Reese asked. Reese probably walked into a 1:1 in October and said "hey, can we make this weekly" and Drew said "sure" and that was that. Reese got the thing by asking for the thing. I never asked because asking would mean acknowledging I wanted more of Drew, which is the whole problem I've been writing around for months.

The anger started to thin out around the edges then. Underneath it was the more familiar thing. Smallness. The bodega-seltzer thing. The version of me who didn't know you could ask for more time with your manager because I was too busy decoding the time I already had. Reese isn't ahead of me because Drew likes Reese more. Reese is ahead of me because Reese behaves like a person who can ask for things. I behave like a person waiting to be given things. Those are different operating systems and they produce different calendars.

But — and this is the part the floor spot is for — the anger wasn't entirely off-base, either. I want to keep some of it. Not the part aimed at Reese, which is just jealousy in a costume. The part aimed at Drew is a little embarrassing but it's also — Drew could have offered. Drew is the manager. Drew presumably knows that some employees ask for things and some don't, and the ones who don't ask are not always the ones who don't need. I have done good work for Drew. The diagram. The clean doc. Drew apologized to me on a Saturday for needing a file. Drew clearly doesn't think I'm bad at this. Drew also hasn't offered me weekly time. Both can be true. I'm allowed to be a little annoyed about the second one even while I admit I never asked.

What did I do with it in the next hour. Sat on the floor for maybe thirty minutes. Got up. Made eggs again — apparently eggs are my floor-recovery food now. Texted Avery a thumbs down, no context. Avery sent back "?" and I said "long story, not bad just annoyed, tell you later." Which was — different. Usually I would have said "fine." Tonight I said "annoyed." Small thing. Counts.

Then I opened a draft Slack to Drew. Not to send tonight. Just a draft. It said: "hey, when we have our 1:1 next week, can we talk about cadence? wondering if it'd make sense to do these weekly." I read it three times. The lowercase. The "wondering if it'd make sense." It sounds like me. It also sounds like a person asking for a thing. I left it in drafts. I'll look at it tomorrow. Maybe send it. Maybe rewrite it smaller. But I wrote it, which I wouldn't have done last month.

Plant has ten leaves. I counted on the way past. The simmer is mostly cool. The draft is still there.

entries.prompt: When was the last time you felt genuinely proud of yourself — not the kind that needs anyone else to confirm it, just a private one? Tell me where you were when you noticed it and what you'd just done.
entries.content:
Friday morning, on the bus. Around 8:40. The 14 was running late and the windows were fogged and I was in my usual seat, the back-right one, with my bag on my lap.

I sent the message to Drew. Not the draft from Thursday night — I rewrote it a little. Took out "wondering if it'd make sense to" because it sounded like I was asking permission to ask. The version I sent was: "hey — quick thing for our next 1:1, want to talk about making these weekly instead of biweekly. think it'd help me keep things in motion a bit better." I read it once and hit send before my hand could find another tab to open.

And then — and this is the part I want to write down — I just put my phone away. I put it in my bag. Not face-down on my lap. Not in my pocket where I could feel it. In the bag, zipped. And I looked out the window for the rest of the ride.

That's it. That's the moment.

I didn't watch for the typing dots. I didn't refresh. I didn't compose imaginary versions of Drew's reply. I didn't pre-load defenses for the version where Drew says "let's stick with biweekly for now." I just — sent the thing and let it be sent. The bus kept going. People got on at the stop by the park. A guy with a guitar case sat across from me and the guitar case had a sticker on it that said BORN TO LOSE in like 1970s biker font and I thought that was funny and didn't take a picture of it for anyone, just thought it was funny by myself.

I realized somewhere around the next stop that I felt — okay. Not great. Not vindicated. Just okay. Like I'd done a small, normal thing that small, normal employees do all the time, which is ask their manager for a thing they want. And the world had not ended in the eight minutes between sending and not-checking.

And then the proud thing came. Not loud. It was actually really quiet. Just a small recognition somewhere in my chest, like — huh. You did that. You wrote the draft last night, you let it sit, you rewrote it smaller this morning, you sent it on the bus, you put the phone away. That's a whole sequence. That's a person doing a thing. That's not me-from-October. Me-from-October would have stared at the draft for three days, then deleted it, then complained about Reese's 1:1 cadence into the floor by the chair for a month.

I didn't tell anyone. That's part of why I think it counts. I didn't text Avery "I just sent Drew a thing!" with exclamation points and wait for the validation. I didn't think about how I'd describe it to Mom on Tuesday. I just sat on the bus with it. Mine. Quiet.

I want to be careful not to inflate this because the inflating would be its own kind of audition — look how grown I am for asking for a 1:1, give me a sticker. The thing isn't huge. Plenty of people send harder messages every day before breakfast. What's mine about it is just that for me, this one, sent like that, in that order, with the phone in the bag after — that's a piece of evidence about who I might be becoming. Someone who can ask. Someone who can ask and then not stand outside the door waiting for the answer.

Drew replied at like 11:30. Said "yeah, totally, let's do it. I'll update the recurring." Three sentences with the "totally" in there. No drama. No "interesting that you asked" or "is everything okay." Just yes. The reply landed and I noticed something — I didn't screenshot it. I read it, said "thanks!" back, and went back to what I was doing. Like a normal email exchange between a normal manager and a normal employee. Which is, I'm starting to realize, what most of the actual interactions have been the whole time. I just wasn't letting them be that.

The proud thing got a little quieter after the reply, weirdly. Or maybe not weirdly. Because the proud part wasn't about getting the yes. The proud part was about sending it without already knowing the answer. The yes was almost beside the point. If Drew had said "let's stick with biweekly, here's why" I think I'd have been okay. Disappointed for a minute, sure. But the thing I'd done — the asking — would still have been the thing I did. The yes didn't make the asking real. The asking was already real on the bus, before any reply existed.

That's new. That feels new. Usually the thing I do isn't the thing — the thing is the thing's reception. I do something and then wait for the verdict and the verdict becomes the actual event in my head, the only part I remember. This time the bus ride is the part I remember. The fogged window. The BORN TO LOSE sticker. The phone in the bag. Me looking out at the river when we crossed it, just looking, no one watching.

I also sent the Hannah picture, by the way. Not Friday. Wednesday morning. I didn't write about it because — I think I wanted to see if she'd reply before I made it a thing, and also because I was a little superstitious about writing it down before it happened. She replied in nine minutes. She said "I AM SCREAMING the leg is BACKWARDS" and then "wait hi how are you" and I sent the plant photo and said "she has nine leaves now." We've been texting a little since. Not catch-up dance. Just sending things. So that's also a thing I did, and I didn't make it a parade, and I'm proud of that too in the same quiet way. Different bus, same shape.

Plant is at ten now. The asking-Drew thing happened. The Hannah thing is happening. The bus crossed the river and I was just on it.

That's the entry. Quietly mine.

entries.prompt: Last week the Reese-calendar discovery, the Drew message, the Hannah text — there's been a lot of work-and-being-seen running through the entries. Set that aside for a second. When was the last time you were alone in the apartment with nothing you had to do, and how did you spend the first hour?
entries.content:
Sunday, last Sunday. Avery was at a wedding upstate, left Saturday morning, didn't get back till Monday night. So Sunday I had the whole apartment to myself, a real day, no work, no plans, nothing on the calendar. I'd been kind of looking forward to it all week in this slightly weird way, like I was going to be a good steward of the time. Going to read. Going to clean the chair pile. Going to make a real breakfast. Going to maybe do the savings thing finally because nobody was around to see me struggle through the password reset.

Here's what I actually did in the first hour.

Woke up at like 9:15, which is late for me. The apartment was quiet in a way it isn't usually — Avery isn't loud, but Avery being not-there is its own kind of sound. I lay in bed for a minute and noticed I hadn't reached for the phone. Not because I was being good. Because there was no one to check on. Drew doesn't text on Sundays. The group chat had been quiet. There was just — nothing waiting. So I lay there and looked at the ceiling for a while, which has a small water stain near the corner I've never really registered before, sort of an oblong shape, kind of like Italy if Italy were doing okay.

Then I got up and made coffee. Not the good kind, the regular kind, the cheap stuff in the freezer. Stood at the counter while it brewed. The plant was getting morning light, the leaves doing the thing where they look almost translucent, and I just stood there with my hands on the counter looking at it. Eleven leaves now. I counted twice to make sure. I was wearing the same t-shirt I'd slept in and my hair was doing something and nobody was going to see me and that felt — I don't know. It felt like the apartment was mine in a way it usually isn't. Usually the apartment is a place I'm passing through between work and bed, or a place Avery and I are doing things in together. This morning it was just a room with me in it.

I took the coffee back to bed. I know. I had a whole list. I went back to bed with the coffee and opened my laptop and watched two episodes of a show I've already seen, the one about the bakery, the comfort one. Didn't even pick a new show. Picked the one I knew. Episodes I could half-watch because I knew what was going to happen.

That was the first hour. Coffee in bed. Show I'd seen. Phone face-down on the comforter, picked up twice to check the time, put back down.

Here's what I want to be honest about. I felt a little guilty during it. Like I was wasting the alone-day. The list was right there in my head — the chair, the savings, the real breakfast — and I was instead in bed at 10am rewatching a show about people making bread. The guilt voice was running. You have a whole day and you're already burning the front of it on this. Avery would be doing something with this time. Avery would have gone for a run.

But the guilt didn't quite catch. That's what was different. Usually the guilt would have gotten me out of bed and into a different kind of doing — productive doing, list doing — that I would have half-finished and then felt worse about. This time I just noticed the guilt and kept watching the bakery show. Like the guilt was a noise from the next apartment over. Yeah, I hear you, but I'm doing this.

What I think was happening, sitting here writing about it now, is that I was tired in a way I hadn't let myself be in a while. Not tired-tired. The deeper kind. The kind that builds up under all the scanning. The Drew-message week, the Hannah back-and-forth, the simmer about the calendar — even the good ones cost something. And alone in the apartment with no one to perform for, no Avery to be a person around, no Slack to check (I really did not check Slack, not in that first hour, not until like 1pm), the body just kind of — sagged. Into the bed. Into the show. Into the coffee.

I didn't do the chair. I never did the chair. I did, eventually, around 4pm, do one thing from the list — I reset the savings password. Just that. Got the email, clicked the link, made a new password, logged in for two seconds, saw the number, closed the tab. The number was — fine? I think? It wasn't bad. It wasn't impressive. It was a number. I didn't do anything with it. Didn't increase the contribution, didn't budget, didn't do the life-audit. Just looked. Closed it. Considered that a full afternoon's work. Which, by the standards of the morning version of me with the list, was a failure, and by the standards of two months ago when I couldn't even open the login screen, was actually something.

But the first hour. The first hour was the bakery show and the coffee and the t-shirt and the water stain that looks like Italy. And the thing I want to write down before I lose it is that the first hour was, I think, the most honest hour I've had in a while. No one to be for. No version of me to maintain. Just a person in a bed watching a show. That's apparently what I do when no one's looking. Not the productive version. Not the disciplined version. Not the spiraling version either, interestingly. Just — a person who likes coffee and bakery shows and being warm.

I kept expecting the loneliness to show up. The thing where being alone in the apartment turns into being alone in a bigger sense, the bodega-seltzer feeling, the smallness. It didn't, that first hour. It came later, around dinner, a little, and I ordered Thai and texted Avery a picture of the food and that was fine. But the morning was just morning. Quiet, lit, warm, mine.

Eleven leaves. Italy on the ceiling. The bakery, again.

entries.prompt: Who's someone in your life you've been meaning to reach out to but haven't — not Drew, not Hannah, someone else? Tell me the last time they crossed your mind and what you were doing when it happened.
entries.content:
Ben. Has to be Ben. Of the three of them — Nat, Ben, Priya — Ben is the one I keep half-thinking about and never quite reaching. Nat sends things to the group, Priya I've been a little better about because of the birthday thing, but Ben has gone the quietest, and so I've gone quiet right back at him, and the silence between us has its own small weight now.

He crossed my mind on Saturday. I was at the laundromat again — I keep ending up at the laundromat for these little realizations, it's apparently my unscheduled-thoughts venue — and I had headphones in, and a song came on shuffle that he and I used to play in his car senior year. Not even a song I'd say is my favorite. Just one of those songs that lived in a specific car on specific drives. We used to do a thing where he'd play the opening and I'd guess the song before the vocals came in, and I never got this one, every single time he played it I forgot what it was, and he thought that was hilarious. The intro started in the laundromat and I knew it instantly, which would have annoyed him in the funniest way.

I sat on the bench, the same bench where I'd thought about Hannah, and let the song play. Didn't skip it. And there he was — Ben, twenty-one, in the driver's seat, one hand on the wheel, the other doing the dumb little point at the speakers when the chorus hit. And then twenty-four Ben, who I don't really know. Who I've seen exactly once since graduation, at a thing in someone else's city, where we hugged and said we should hang out and didn't.

What's different about Ben from Hannah, I think, is that with Hannah the silence was passive — we both stopped, neither of us pushed, and the friendship just sort of sat down. With Ben there's a thing I'm avoiding. He's been kind of struggling. Not in any big visible way. But the last real conversation we had was on the phone maybe a year ago, where he said, in this offhand way that wasn't really offhand, that he hated his job and didn't know what he was doing and felt stuck, and I said the things you say — that sounds rough, you'll figure it out, want to get on a call this weekend — and we didn't get on a call that weekend, and I haven't followed up. Once. In a year. He's been carrying whatever he's carrying and I have not asked him about it again.

That's the thing. The thing I'm walking around. With Hannah the dread was finding out we don't fit. With Ben the dread is that he might actually need something, and I might not know how to give it, or I might give it badly, or I might give it and then have to keep giving it, and I have already failed once at being the person on the other end of his struggling and I don't know how to come back from that failure except to pretend it didn't happen, which is what I've been doing.

The math I keep running is — Ben hasn't reached out to me either. Which I tell myself means he's fine, or he's busy, or he doesn't need me, or whatever version of "I'm off the hook" I can construct. But I know what it actually means, because it's what my own silence means: he probably tried once, didn't get what he needed, and stopped trying. That's the more likely version. And I have been hiding inside the easier interpretation for a year because the harder one would require me to do something.

What I was doing when he crossed my mind — folding a towel badly, the song in my ears, the laundromat fluorescents doing their thing. And I got this flicker of the same feeling I had with Hannah, the same doorway shape, but heavier. Because Hannah was a friendship I'd let fade. Ben is a friend I'd kind of left. Those are different. The Hannah text was a stupid photo because the friendship could carry stupid. I don't think a stupid photo would do it for Ben. The reentry has to be different. It has to acknowledge something.

I've been drafting a message in my head ever since. Nothing I'd send. Just versions. "Hey, I've been thinking about you, sorry I went quiet, how are you really." Too much. "Hey, miss you, drink soon when I'm in your city?" Too little — also a lie, I have no plans to be in his city. "That song came on at the laundromat and I thought of you, also I'm sorry I didn't follow up after our last call, I should have." That one is closer. That one has the apology in it. The apology is the thing that has to be there. Without it the message is me asking for the friendship back without acknowledging the part where I dropped it.

I'm scared of the apology because the apology asks for a reply that's bigger than a stupid photo would. The apology opens a real conversation. He could say "yeah, that hurt" or "yeah, where were you" or even just "it's fine, don't worry about it" in a way that means it isn't fine. All three of those are things I'd have to sit with.

But the alternative is what I've been doing, which is letting Ben be a contact I think about at laundromats and don't reach. Letting the song be a small private grief. Letting the year become two years become however long it takes for the apology to be too late to make.

I'm not going to send it tonight. I know better than to do this on the floor at 10pm. But I'm going to draft it tomorrow. Real draft. Not in-my-head draft. Open the messages, type the thing, leave it. Then look at it Wednesday. Then maybe Thursday I send it, on a bus, like the Drew thing, and put the phone away.

That's the plan. The half-second of friction. The face-down. We'll see.

Plant has eleven still. Ben would think the leaf-counting is funny. He'd take it too far, ask me to name them. He'd be right to.

entries.prompt: When was the last time you felt embarrassed — even mildly, even just to yourself? Tell me where you were, what happened, and what you did in the next few minutes.
entries.content:
Yesterday at the coffee place. The one with the barista who knows my order. Septum ring. I've been going there for like a year and a half now and the whole bit is established — I walk in, she nods, I do my little hesitation about the oat milk, she does her little eyebrow thing, I cave or don't cave, we move on. It's a routine. It's nice. It's one of the four or five places in this city where I feel like a person who has a place.

So yesterday I walked in and she said "hey!" and I said "hi, can I get a —" and she said "Kyle, right? medium oat?" And smiled.

Kyle.

Kyle.

I have been going there for a year and a half and she thinks my name is Kyle.

I want to be clear about what happened next because the next two seconds were a whole little movie. First — and this is the embarrassing part, the actually embarrassing part — I said "yeah, that's me." I said yes. I confirmed I was Kyle. I watched my own mouth do it and I couldn't stop it. She wrote Kyle on the cup with a Sharpie and I stood there watching her write the wrong name on my coffee like I was witnessing a small crime I had committed.

Then she handed me my card back and I went and stood in the pickup area and the heat in my face was — I felt it everywhere. My ears. My neck. The back of my scalp. Because the math was happening in real time. How long has she thought I was Kyle. Has she been calling me Kyle this whole time and I haven't been listening when she calls the order. Has she said "thanks Kyle" before and I just said "thanks" back without registering. Probably yes. Almost certainly yes. I am the kind of person who would not register the wrong name at a coffee shop because I'm too busy in my head.

And the worse part — the actually worse part — is that I now have to either be Kyle forever or have a confrontation. Like, the next time I go in, what do I do? Do I say "actually it's Kai"? After a year and a half of being Kyle? She's going to feel terrible. She's going to do that thing where she apologizes too much and then it's weird and then I can't go there anymore. Or do I just become Kyle. Do I just let it happen. Let her keep writing Kyle on the cup, let her say "see you later Kyle" when I leave, build a whole shadow life as Kyle the medium oat guy who comes in three times a week.

I picked up the coffee. The cup said Kyle in blue Sharpie. I left. I didn't even look at her on the way out, I just pretended to be very interested in adjusting my bag.

Walking to the bus I went through it again. The "yeah, that's me." Why. Why did you say that. Why did you not just gently correct her. Normal people correct baristas. Normal people say "oh actually it's Kai" with a small laugh and the barista says "oh god I'm so sorry" and they both move on and it's a nothing moment. That was right there. That was the available exit. And I picked the door marked "agree to be Kyle indefinitely" instead.

What was running underneath it, I think, was — I didn't want her to feel bad. That's the surface answer. I didn't want to embarrass her. I made a calculation in less than half a second that her embarrassment would cost more than my misnaming, and I just absorbed the misnaming. That's the polite-lie reflex again. The Dad call. The "totally great." Always picking the version that costs the other person nothing, even when the cost to me is — apparently — losing my own name at my own coffee shop.

But also, partly, I don't think I felt entitled to be named correctly. That's the weirder thing to write. Like, in the half-second, some part of me went — well, you've been coming here for a year and a half and you've never once introduced yourself, you've just been a transactional silhouette to her, of course she made up a name based on whatever she half-heard once, that's on you, that's what you get. As if the wrong name was a fair consequence of having been quiet.

On the bus I had the cup on my lap and I kept looking at the Kyle and feeling the heat come back. It came in waves. I'd be looking out the window and forget about it for a minute and then I'd see the cup and feel it again, the prickle behind the ears. I texted Avery a picture of the cup with just "kyle." Avery wrote back "?????" and then "WAIT WHAT" and then "you have to fix this" and I said "I think I'm Kyle now" and Avery sent like seven laughing emojis and "absolutely not, you're going back, this is unacceptable." Avery's outrage made me laugh out loud on the bus, real laugh, not polite, and the embarrassment uncoiled a little around the edges.

I'm not going to fix it today. I'm going to think about how to fix it for probably four more days, and then on Friday or Saturday I'll go in and order and when she says "Kyle?" I'll say, very lightly, "actually it's Kai, sorry, I should have said something ages ago, my fault." Putting it on me. So she gets the easy out. So nobody has to hold the embarrassment alone.

Or I'll chicken out and be Kyle forever. We'll see.

Plant has twelve leaves. Twelve leaves and a barista who calls me Kyle. The accounting continues.

entries.prompt: When was the last time you said something out loud that you'd been rehearsing in your head — a small speech, a thing you'd been planning to bring up, a line you'd practiced? Tell me where you were, who you said it to, and how it actually came out compared to the version you'd run.
entries.content:
Tuesday. The first weekly 1:1 with Drew. The new cadence I'd asked for. I'd been carrying around a thing I wanted to say in it for like four days — basically since Drew confirmed the recurring change, I'd started building this little speech in my head about why I'd asked. Because I figured Drew would ask. Drew would say "so what made you want weekly" and I would have to have a real answer, and the real answer couldn't be "because I saw on your calendar that Reese has weekly and I had a small breakdown about it on the floor by my chair." The real answer had to be a professional version of that.

So I'd been working on it. On the bus, mostly. In the shower. While brushing my teeth. The line was something like — "I think with the project ramping up there's stuff I want to flag earlier rather than waiting two weeks, and I'd also like a more regular space to talk about how I'm developing in the role, not just status updates." That was the one I'd settled on. I said it out loud to the mirror Monday night. It sounded fine. It sounded like a sentence a competent person would say. I'd added "developing in the role" specifically because it sounded like a phrase from a performance review, like a thing managers like to hear, and I'd liked how it tasted in my mouth.

The 1:1 was at 2pm. I got there a minute early, which is what I always do, and Drew was already in the room, which is rare. Drew had a coffee. Drew said "hey, how's your week" and I said "good, busy, you know" and sat down.

And then — Drew didn't ask. Drew didn't ask why I'd wanted weekly. Drew just opened the doc we share, the running notes one, and said "okay, what's on your mind." Like every other 1:1. Like the cadence change wasn't a thing, like we'd always done this, like Drew hadn't even particularly registered the ask as a meaningful event. Which makes sense, probably. From Drew's side it was a Slack that said "yeah, totally, let's do it" and a calendar update. It wasn't a moment for Drew. It was a moment for me.

So I sat there with my whole rehearsed sentence loaded in my mouth and nowhere to put it.

What I did — and this is the part I want to write down — I said it anyway. Not in response to a question. Just — into the silence after "what's on your mind." I said, "actually, before we get into the list, I wanted to say — part of why I asked for weekly was that I want to be able to flag things earlier instead of letting them sit two weeks, and I'd also like to use some of the time to talk about, like, how I'm growing in this role. Not just status."

I did not say "developing in the role." When it actually came out, "developing" felt like a costume word and my mouth grabbed "growing" instead, which was less polished but more mine. I noticed the swap as I was making it, like watching someone else drive my car. Also, I'd had a clean two-clause version in my head and what came out had an "actually" at the front and a "like" in the middle and ended with a comma instead of a period — "Not just status" hanging there, half a sentence, a little limp.

Drew nodded. Took a second. Said "yeah, that makes sense. I'd actually been thinking we should talk more about that stuff. Good." Then opened the doc and said "okay so where are we on the cross-team thing."

That was it. That was the whole transaction. The thing I'd rehearsed for four days got delivered in maybe eleven seconds, with verbal stumbles, and Drew said "good" and we moved on.

And here's what's strange. I wasn't disappointed. I thought I would be — I'd built it up in my head as this little summit, and it ended up being a thing I said while Drew was opening a tab. But walking back to my desk afterward, what I felt was relief, not deflation. Because the rehearsed version had become its own pressure, this thing I had to land cleanly or I'd "wasted" the moment. And the actual version had been imperfect and immediate and Drew had taken it like a normal piece of information, not a performance, and the fact that it wasn't a summit was actually — fine. It wasn't supposed to be a summit. I'd made it one in my head because I make everything with Drew into a summit.

The rehearsing did do something, though. I want to be honest about that. The fact that I'd rehearsed meant I had a real sentence available when there was a half-second opening. If I hadn't rehearsed, when Drew said "what's on your mind," I would've defaulted to the project list and the moment for saying the bigger thing would have closed. The rehearsal got me to the door. It just didn't deliver me through it the way I'd planned. The actual passage was scrappier — an "actually" hung on the front, a "growing" instead of a "developing," a comma where I'd planned a period. I think the scrappiness was probably what made it sound like me. Drew's "good" was, I think, partly a response to that. Not the polished version. The me-shaped version.

I sat down at my desk and wrote in the running notes doc: "discussed cadence rationale — flag earlier, growth conversations." Bullet point. Two phrases. The four days of rehearsing reduced to nine words I'll see again next week. That seems about right. The thing wasn't the speech. The thing was that I said it.

I texted Avery on the walk home. Just "did the 1:1, said the thing about weekly, was fine." Avery sent back a heart and "knew you would." I noticed I didn't feel the need to elaborate. The thing was already filed. Quietly mine, again. Same shape as the bus.

Plant has twelve still. The barista, when I went in this morning, called me Kyle. I let it go again. One thing at a time.

entries.prompt: When you got home yesterday and shut the door behind you, what was the first thing you did, and what was the thing you were avoiding doing instead?
entries.content:
Yesterday. Got home around 6:40. Shut the door behind me, did the lock thing, dropped the keys in the bowl. Took my shoes off. Stood in the entryway for a second longer than I needed to, like I was waiting to see which version of the evening was going to claim me.

The first thing I did was go to the kitchen and open the fridge. Not because I was hungry. Because the fridge is a thing you can open that gives you a little visual input without asking anything of you. I stood there with the door open looking at — half a lemon, a thing of yogurt, the leftover pasta from Sunday in a container that's been there long enough to be a question, a beer Avery left. I didn't take anything out. I just looked. Then I closed the fridge and stood at the counter for a second, hands on the edge of it, and thought about what I was actually doing.

What I was avoiding was the message from Ben.

He'd replied. Sometime in the afternoon, around 3, I'd seen the notification on my watch in a meeting and not opened it. Just saw his name and the start of the message — "hey man" — and locked the screen and went back to the meeting. Then for the rest of the day I'd known there was a message from Ben sitting in the messages app, unread, and I had not opened it. Not at my desk. Not on the bus. Not on the walk from the bus stop. The phone had been in my hand or my pocket the whole time and I had not looked.

This is — okay. I had drafted the apology message Tuesday like I said I would. I did the thing. Real draft, real apology, a paragraph that took me about forty minutes to get to a version I could stand. I sent it Wednesday morning, on the bus, same as the Drew one — phone in the bag after, didn't watch for the reply. That part went fine. I felt the same quiet thing.

But then the reply didn't come for a day and a half. And the quiet thing started turning. By yesterday morning I'd built a whole thing about what the silence meant. By yesterday afternoon when his name finally popped up I had spent so long imagining the contents of the reply that I couldn't bring myself to read the actual one. Like, the imagined Bens were so loud — angry Ben, polite-distant Ben, "it's fine don't worry about it" Ben who means it isn't, Ben who'd moved on so completely my apology was almost embarrassing to him — that the real one couldn't compete. The real one would just be one Ben. The imagined ones were a chorus.

So I stood at the counter in my socks and did the thing. Pulled the phone out. Looked at the lock screen. Saw "hey man" again. Did not open it.

What I did instead — I made tea. I never make tea. There's tea in the cabinet from when Avery had a cold in November. I put the kettle on. I got a mug down. I picked the chamomile. I stood there watching the kettle, which is its own kind of avoiding because I could have just opened the message in the time it took the water to heat. Then I poured the water. Then I let it steep too long, like four full minutes, because I was using the steeping as a kind of clock for how long I could legitimately not be looking at my phone.

Then I took the tea to the floor by the chair. Of course. The radiator was on. I sat with my back against it and the tea between my hands and the phone face-down next to me on the floorboards. And I sat there, just breathing, for I don't know how long. Maybe ten minutes. Long enough that the tea got drinkable.

The thing I want to write down is — I knew, the whole time, that the not-reading was the avoiding. I wasn't tricking myself. I wasn't telling myself "I'll read it after dinner" or "I just need a minute." I was sitting on the floor, fully aware that there was a message from a person I love that I'd asked to write to me, that I had specifically opened a door for, and I was choosing to not look at the answer. The whole game of the past week — the apology, the friction, the bus ride, the phone in the bag — all of it had been about the asking. I hadn't budgeted for the answering. I'd somehow imagined the proud thing again, the bus thing, where the asking was the event and the reply was beside the point. But Ben isn't a 1:1 cadence. Ben is a person. The reply is not beside the point. The reply is the point.

I got it eventually. After the tea. I picked up the phone, sat up a little straighter against the radiator, and opened it.

He wrote — and I'm not going to put the whole thing here because some of it isn't mine — but the gist was: thank you. He said thank you a lot. He said he'd been waiting, kind of, without knowing he was waiting. He said the year had been hard in ways he hadn't told anyone, including me, and that he understood why I went quiet because he'd gone quiet too, and that no apology was needed but he was going to take it anyway because it was nice. He asked if we could get on a call this weekend. He sent a picture of his cat, who I didn't know he had.

I cried a little. The chamomile kind of cry, not the floor kind. Brief. Then I texted him back yes, this weekend, name a time. Then I sat on the floor for another minute with the phone in my lap.

What I'd been avoiding wasn't Ben being mad. It was Ben being kind. Same shape as the bodega seltzer. The kindness undoes me more than the bad thing would have. I can survive somebody being short with me. The thing I can't quite handle is somebody handing me grace I didn't earn cleanly.

Plant has thirteen. I noticed when I got up to put the mug in the sink. Thirteen and a cat I'm going to meet on FaceTime Saturday.

entries.prompt: Think about a stretch of time recently — an evening, a weekend afternoon, a commute — where you were mostly with your own thoughts and nothing was demanding anything of you. What were you actually thinking about, and where did your mind keep drifting back to?
entries.content:
Thursday evening. Avery had a thing — some work dinner that ran late — and I'd taken the long way home from the bus stop on purpose, three extra blocks, because the weather had finally turned and it was that specific kind of evening where the light goes orange and people are sitting on their stoops again like they just remembered they could. I didn't have headphones in. I'd left them on my desk, accidentally, and instead of being annoyed about it I just walked.

What I was actually thinking about was — okay, let me try to be honest, because the easy version is "nothing, I was just walking, it was nice." That's not true. My head was moving the whole time. It just wasn't moving in the usual way.

First few blocks I was kind of replaying the call with Ben on Saturday. Not the content. Just the texture of it. The fact that we'd talked for an hour and forty minutes and at one point he'd put his cat on the screen and the cat had walked across the keyboard and disconnected the call and we'd both laughed about it when we got back on. The fact that hearing his actual voice — not the imagined chorus, the real one — had been kind of staggering for the first ten minutes and then become normal again so fast it scared me. Like the year and a half hadn't actually broken anything. Like the friendship had just been sitting in a closet and we opened the closet and it was still there, slightly dusty, still ours. I kept turning that over. The fact that it had still been there. The fact that I'd been so sure it wouldn't be.

Then somewhere around the park I drifted into thinking about the barista. Still Kyle. I went in this morning and she said "Kyle?" and I said "yeah, thanks" and walked out, again, with my own wrong name on a cup. I thought about it on the walk home and I realized — I'm not going to fix it. I think I've decided. I'm going to be Kyle there. Not because I can't say the thing. I could say the thing. It's that the wrong name has somehow become its own little private joke with myself, a thing only I know, and correcting it would close the joke. Which is — I'm not sure if that's growth or some new costume. Probably both. The Kyle thing is going to become a story I tell, eventually. The barista who called me Kyle for two years.

Then for a while I wasn't thinking about anything specific. That's the part I want to write down. There was a stretch — maybe five blocks, maybe seven — where I was just walking and noticing things. A guy with two enormous dogs that were clearly walking him. A kid on a scooter going way too fast with a parent jogging behind. The smell of someone grilling on a fire escape somewhere, which I'm pretty sure isn't legal but is one of the best things about this city in spring. The way one tree on my block is way ahead of the others, like it didn't get the memo, already half-leafed when the others are still bare. I kept noticing things and not reaching for the phone to photograph any of them, which is — I don't know when I started doing that. The not-reaching. Some time in the last few weeks. The bus rides without checking. The first hour on Sunday. The phone in the bag after the Drew message. It might be becoming a thing.

But then — and this is the part I keep circling — somewhere around my own block, when I could see my building, my mind started drifting back, again, to the same place it's been drifting for a couple weeks now. Which is just — the question of whether I'm okay. Not in a panicked way. In a kind of curious way. Like I keep poking at it from different angles trying to figure out the answer.

Because something has shifted. I can feel it. The Drew thing isn't four hundred pounds anymore. It's still maybe two hundred. But I sent the cadence message. I went to the 1:1 and said the growing-in-the-role thing. I have not screenshotted a Slack message in three weeks. Reese is still ahead of me at most things and I have noticed I am less actively gnawed on by it. Hannah and I are texting. Ben is on the other end of a phone again. The plant is at thirteen leaves, which is absurd, that plant was dying when I started writing in this thing. Avery said something the other day about how I seem "less in my head lately" and I noticed I didn't deflect it, I just said "yeah, maybe."

But also — I had a bad Tuesday, the one with Mom, where I edited everything I said to her, and I sat there afterward feeling that old smallness. And I still check Slack first thing in the morning a lot of the time, the phone-face-down thing works maybe two days out of seven. And I let the barista call me Kyle. And I haven't called Dad back.

So what my mind kept drifting back to, walking those last two blocks in the orange light, was — I think I'm okay. I think I might actually be okay, in the way Mom was sort of asking about months ago and I couldn't answer. Not okay in the brochure sense. Okay in the sense that the scaffolding I thought was so thin has been quietly accumulating. Months and a plant and some people who are nice to me. It might be that's what it looks like when it's working. It might just be months, slowly turning into a life, while you're busy being scared.

I got to my building. Stood outside for a second before going in. The orange was starting to go. There was nobody inside waiting for me, no Avery, no plans, just an apartment and a plant and a couch. I went up. I made eggs. I didn't open Slack until this morning. Thirteen leaves and one tree on my block already remembering what it is.

entries.prompt: Last time someone gave you something — a compliment, a favor, an offer of help, a small kindness — and you found yourself deflecting it or moving past it quickly. Where were you, what did they say, and what did you do with it?
entries.content:
Monday at work.
