The coffee thing in the kitchen — there was a little plate of cookies someone had brought in, the kind from the bakery down the block, and I was getting water and not thinking about anything in particular, and Reese came in and said, "oh hey, I've been meaning to tell you — the framing you did on the timeline doc, the version with the dependencies broken out? I straight up copied that structure for the thing I'm putting together for the other team. It worked really well. So, thanks."

I said "oh, ha, yeah, glad it was useful" and reached for a cookie I didn't want. Took a bite. Said, with my mouth half full, "honestly that was mostly Drew's idea, I just wrote it up." Which is — that's not even true. Drew didn't have anything to do with that structure. I came up with the structure. I sat with it for a week before I wrote the doc. The "Drew's idea" thing just came out of my mouth like a reflex, like I needed to put the credit somewhere else immediately so Reese wouldn't be holding it out to me with both hands.

Reese kind of paused. Did a small face, the kind where someone is registering something but deciding not to push it. Said "well, however it got there, it helped." Took a cookie too. Asked about my weekend. We did the small-talk thing for another minute and went back to our desks.

I sat down and felt the heat behind my ears that I get now when I notice myself doing the thing in real time. Because I had noticed, this time. Not in the moment exactly, but pretty soon after. Like within thirty seconds of walking back to my desk I was already going — wait. Reese gave you a real specific thing. Reese said your work was good and named the part. And you handed it to Drew. Drew, who didn't write it. Drew, who lives in your head rent-free anyway. You handed Reese's compliment to Drew like a tip.

What I think I was doing — and this is what I keep snagging on — is, the compliment from Reese was almost too direct. Drew's "really clean" was a DM I could screenshot and hold at arm's length. Reese was standing there in a kitchen, looking at me, telling me a specific structural thing I had specifically done was specifically useful enough to copy. There was no static between us. No tone to decode. Just Reese, Reese's face, the words. And I couldn't take it. I could not let the compliment land on me, alone, with Reese watching it land. I had to redirect it. Drew was the nearest place to put it, so I put it there.

Which is — I keep thinking about the Avery thing. The "you're so thoughtful with people" entry. Where I wrote about how I bank compliments from outside the perimeter and discount the ones from inside. Reese is sort of in between. Closer than Drew. Further than Avery. And the closer they get, apparently, the harder it is to just take it. Drew's praise becomes a screenshot. Avery's passes through me. Reese's I deflect to Drew. There's some calibration where the only kind of compliment I can really hold is the kind I can't quite reach.

The thing I want to be honest about is that Reese's was probably the most useful one I've gotten in months. Because it was specific. "The framing with the dependencies broken out." That's a thing I did. That's a thing I could do again, on purpose, knowing it works. Drew's "really clean" I've been turning over for weeks trying to figure out what was clean. Reese just told me. Reese told me exactly what worked and why. And I couldn't take the gift because to take it I'd have to admit I'd done something good on purpose, with intention, that another competent person looked at and learned from. That's a different size of self than I usually let myself have. Apparently still too big. I shrunk it back down by handing it to Drew.

What I did with it after — a couple things. I didn't screenshot anything, for one. Nothing to screenshot, no message. Just a kitchen exchange that's already getting blurry around the edges in my memory. I tried to write down what Reese had actually said while I still had the words, in the running notes doc, just as a line: "Reese said the dependency-breakout structure worked well, copied it for [other team] thing." Just so it would exist somewhere in writing. Evidence, in case I deflate it by Wednesday. Which I will.

Then later, around 4, I almost went over to Reese's desk to say — I don't know — something. Something like "hey, by the way, that wasn't actually Drew's idea, I should have just said thanks." A correction. Closing the loop. I didn't go. I rehearsed it twice and didn't go. Because correcting it would mean acknowledging that I'd deflected, which would mean Reese knowing I'd deflected, which felt like more exposure than just letting the moment pass. So instead I'm writing about it here, where only I have to look at it. Same shape as a hundred other things. The honest version goes in the notebook. The performed version goes in the room.

But — and I want to write this down too, because I think it counts — I noticed. Within thirty seconds. Not three days later, not on the floor, not in some retrospective reckoning. Thirty seconds. That's faster than I used to be. The reflex still beat me, but I caught it on the way back. And the next time Reese says something like that, maybe I get to the catching faster. Maybe one of these times I just say "thanks, that means a lot" and stop. Stop talking. Don't add the disclaimer. Don't reroute it. Just let it sit on me for a second like it's mine.

Plant has fourteen. I'm starting to think it might be a different plant than the one I started with.

entries.prompt: There's a thing that happened this week where afterward you wished you'd said something different — not necessarily wrong, just not quite the thing. Tell me where you were, what you actually said, and what the truer version would have been.
entries.content:
Wednesday night. Avery's kitchen. Avery was making pasta — the lazy version, the one with the jarred sauce that Avery doctors up so it tastes like it didn't come from a jar — and I was sitting at the counter with a glass of wine, and we were talking about nothing, and then Avery turned around with the wooden spoon in hand and said, kind of out of nowhere, "do you ever think about, like, what we're doing? long term?"

Just like that. Wooden spoon, sauce on it, eight months in, Wednesday pasta.

What I said was — "ha, what, like, marriage?" Which, first of all, I do not know why marriage was the first word out of my mouth. Avery had not said marriage. Avery had said long term. There's a whole continent between long term and marriage and I airdropped us into the deep end before Avery had even finished the sentence. And then I said, fast, before Avery could answer, "I mean, yeah, sometimes, I don't know, do you?" Which put it back on Avery. Hot potato. Question goes in, question comes out, and now Avery has to answer the thing Avery had been trying to ask me.

Avery did the small face. The one where Avery is registering something but deciding whether to push. Then Avery said, "I just mean — I think about it. Us. I like us. I was wondering if you do too." And stirred the sauce.

I said "yeah, of course, I think about us all the time." Which is true. Then I made a joke about whether Avery was proposing with a wooden spoon, and Avery laughed, and the moment kind of dissolved. We ate pasta. We watched something. I slept over. It was fine.

But.

What I actually wanted to say — and I knew it within about ninety seconds of saying the wrong thing, the way I always know now — was something more like: yes, I think about it, and the thinking scares me a little, and the scared part isn't about you, it's about me, about whether I can be a person someone builds a long thing with, because I don't always know how to be a person who has a today, let alone a five-years. That's what I wanted to say. Or some version of that. Even just — "yes, I think about it, and I'm glad you do too." Even that. Five words. I had five words available and I picked the marriage joke instead.

I'm trying to figure out what the marriage thing was. Because it really did just leap out. I think — the second Avery said "long term" my whole nervous system went off, because long term is a thing I do not let myself plan for, and the way I've apparently been managing it is by not putting language on any of the in-between. Avery and I have been together eight months. I do not know what month nine looks like in my own head, much less month twenty. The future of us is, in my mind, this kind of soft fog where I don't look directly. And Avery, just by asking, was pointing a flashlight into the fog, and I — instead of looking with Avery — went, "haha what's at the very end of the fog, is it a wedding," because making the question absurd was easier than answering the actual one. The marriage joke was a deflection. It was the same shape as handing Reese's compliment to Drew. Take the thing that's being offered directly and bounce it off something further away so I don't have to hold it.

The truer version, if I'm being really honest — and I'm only going to write this here, in the floor-spot equivalent of the notebook — is that I'm in love with Avery in a way that scares me. Like, not casually. Not "we've been dating eight months and it's nice." I am in love with Avery and I have been kind of carefully not saying that to myself in those words because once you say it in those words you have to do something with the information, you have to let it shape how you behave, you have to be the kind of person who is actually in this and not just the kind of person who is in it provisionally, until something happens or doesn't. The wooden-spoon question was Avery, very gently, asking me to be the first kind. And I made a marriage joke.

Avery wasn't asking for a vow. Avery was asking if we were doing the same thing. If we were both, like, in the same room facing the same direction. And the answer is yes. I am. I am very much in the same room. I think about Avery's hand on the counter. I think about the lying-A table. I think about waking up at Avery's apartment Sunday morning and the specific way Avery makes coffee, which is — I'm not going to write about how Avery makes coffee right now, I'll lose the thread. The point is, the answer is yes, and what I said was a joke and a redirect, and Avery probably felt the redirect, because Avery is not stupid, Avery felt it, and chose to let it go because Avery is generous and because pasta was getting cold.

I want to bring it back up. That's the thing I keep landing on, sitting on the floor by the chair tonight, Avery not here, the radiator clicking. I want to say — Friday, maybe, if we're at my place — "hey, the other night when you asked about long term, I want to actually answer that." I rehearsed a version of this earlier and it sounded like a memo. Then I tried a smaller version: "I do think about us. I'm glad you asked. The marriage joke was me being nervous." That one feels closer.

I don't know if I'll do it. I know how this goes. I write it here, and Friday comes, and we order Thai, and I don't bring it up, and the moment becomes another thing I sit on. But — Drew got a Slack. Hannah got a photo. Ben got an apology. Maybe Avery gets a Friday sentence. Maybe it comes out scrappier than I plan, with an "actually" on the front and a comma where a period should be, and Avery says "yeah, I know, babe" in the voice that means Avery already knew, and we eat dinner and that's that.

Plant has fifteen. The thing keeps growing whether I notice or not.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel a flash of envy toward someone you actually like — a friend, a coworker, someone whose life is adjacent to yours? Tell me what they have or did that set it off, where you were when it landed, and what you did with it after.
entries.content:
Saturday. The FaceTime with Ben. The one we've been doing every weekend or two since the apology — not on a schedule exactly, just whoever has a free hour reaches out. He called around 4 from his couch, his cat parked on his stomach, and we did the thing we do, which is mostly just hang out on a screen while one of us is doing something else. He was eating a sandwich. I was sort of folding laundry, sort of not.

Then he said, kind of mid-bite, "oh — I forgot to tell you. I got into that program."

The program is a thing he'd mentioned twice, in passing, the kind of thing you pretend isn't a big deal because you don't want to jinx it. A year-long thing. Part-time. A field he's been quietly trying to pivot into for a while, the actual reason behind a lot of the hard year I didn't know about. Competitive. He'd applied in February. I'd half-remembered.

I said "Ben, that's huge, oh my god, congratulations." And I meant it. I did mean it. That part I want to be clear about because what came next was not a contradiction, it was just — also true. I meant the congratulations and I felt the flash. Both at once, in the same chest, no contradiction, both real.

The flash was — he has a thing he's moving toward. He has a direction. The hard year wasn't a hard year of nothing, it was a hard year of him quietly working on something, applying for something, getting rejected from versions of it probably, trying again, and now landing it. He has a thing that, in a year, will make him a different shape than he is now. And I — I have a job I'm doing okay at, a manager I've stopped screenshotting, a relationship I love, a plant. I do not have a direction. I have a present. I do not have a thing I am moving toward.

That was the flash. Right under the collarbone again. The thumb thing.

I kept folding. I asked him about the program — the structure, when it starts, how it works with his job. He told me. He was excited in this restrained way that's how Ben does excited, like he's allowed himself one notch of it and is keeping the rest in reserve in case the universe takes the first notch back. I asked good questions. The cat got bored and left. We hung up after another twenty minutes and I sat on the bed with a half-folded shirt in my lap.

What I did with it after — I want to be honest. First I did the comparison reflex, full speed. Within probably ninety seconds of hanging up I was already cataloging. Ben has a direction. Reese has a direction (allegedly, presumably, calendar-having Reese). Hannah, when we last talked, had mentioned grad school in this maybe-someday voice that I now suspect is a definitely-someday voice. The Jamie-or-Janie person from Mom's call has a house. Everyone in the catalog has, apparently, a thing. I have a — what. I have a current job I am still learning. I have a plant.

The catalog made me feel awful for about ten minutes. I lay back on the bed and stared at the ceiling and the Italy stain and let the awful run.

Then I noticed something I want to write down, because it's new, or new-ish. The awful didn't pull me into the loop the way it usually does. It came in, sat there, and then started thinning out on its own, the way the Reese loop did on the floor that one night. I didn't have to do anything. I just had to not feed it.

And underneath it, when it thinned, was something else. Which was — Ben got into a thing. Ben, who a month ago I hadn't talked to in a year and a half. Ben, who is now on the other end of a Saturday FaceTime with his cat. The thing I'm envious of is happening to a person who is in my life because I sent an apology on a bus. If I hadn't sent the apology I wouldn't even know about the program. I'd have learned about it, eventually, from Nat in the group chat, and felt the flash from much farther away with much less right to it.

The envy is, in a weird way, evidence that the friendship came back. You can't be envious of a stranger's life. You can be a little jealous of an idea of a stranger, but the real thing, the thumb-under-the-collarbone thing, requires proximity. Ben got close enough to envy again. That's the cost of the friendship being real. I'd take that cost. I'd take it ten times.

The other thing I noticed, which I'm sitting with — I don't actually want Ben's program. When I tried to picture myself in it, in the specifics, I didn't want it. I'd be miserable. It's not my field. It's not my shape. What I wanted was the having-a-direction part. The flash wasn't really at Ben. It was at the abstract feature he'd unlocked: he knows what he's working on. I want that feature. I don't want his version of it. Which means the work isn't to get into a program. The work is, eventually, figuring out my own version of forward. Which I do not have to figure out today, which is a sentence I want to repeat to myself because I tend to forget it.

I texted Ben that night. Just "hey, really, I'm so glad. proud of you." Three sentences max. He sent back a heart and "thanks man, means a lot." I didn't add the rest of what I felt because the rest of what I felt was mine to sit with, not his to manage. The congratulations could be clean. The envy could be private. Both true. Both fine.

Plant has sixteen. Italy is still on the ceiling, doing okay.

entries.prompt: Last time you walked into a room — a meeting, a party, a friend's place — and did a quick scan to figure out who you needed to be in there. Where were you, who was in the room, and what version of yourself did you reach for?
entries.content:
Friday night. A bar in Brooklyn for a thing — Avery's coworker's birthday, kind of a low-key thing but bigger than I'd been picturing, like maybe twenty-five people in a back room with a long table and string lights and the music a little too loud. I'd said yes a week ago without really clocking what it was. Showed up at 8:15 with Avery, took my coat off at the door, and did the scan.

The scan happens fast. Like, before I've even located Avery's coworker, the actual birthday person, my eyes have done a sweep of the room and my body has run some kind of reading on it. Who's here. What kind of room is this. Loud or chill. People-I-know zones and stranger zones. Where's the bar. Are people standing or sitting. What are people wearing — which is shorthand for what register the night is in. And by the time I've handed over my coat I have, without consciously deciding to, picked a version.

The version I reached for Friday was — okay, let me try to name it honestly. Funny-but-low-volume Avery's-partner. Quietly witty. A good listener. The kind of person who throws in a line every five minutes that lands and otherwise asks people questions about themselves. Not the center of anything. Definitely not the center of anything. Just — pleasant, present, easy to talk to, no demands. The version that won't embarrass Avery. The version that, if anyone says anything about me to Avery later, it'll be "your partner is lovely" and not anything else.

I want to be clear about the not-embarrassing-Avery thing because I think it's the engine of the whole scan. Avery has been at this job for years. These are Avery's people. I am being introduced and re-introduced and the introductions are, on some level, a representation of Avery's taste in partners. Which means I am being graded as an extension of Avery, which is a kind of grading I take more seriously than my own. I will let myself look bad in front of strangers. I will not let Avery look like the person who brought a weird date.

So I picked the version. Quiet-witty. Asks-questions. And then I did the thing for about ninety minutes. I talked to a guy named Marco about his bike. I talked to two women whose names I caught and immediately lost about a show they were both watching that I haven't seen. I asked good follow-ups about the show, despite not having seen it, because I have learned that you can sustain a five-minute conversation with anyone about anything by just asking the question one layer deeper than they expected. I had a beer. I had another beer. I made one joke about the lights and one comment about the music and otherwise played the side character.

Avery checked on me twice, which I noticed. Once near the bar — "you good?" — and once when I was talking to Marco — Avery just put a hand on my back for a second on the way past. The hand on the back wasn't a check-in exactly. It was something else. A small "I see you" without making me explain what I was being seen doing.

Around 9:30 something happened that I want to write down. The birthday person — Avery's coworker, whose name is Jess — sat down next to me on the bench-seat side of the table and said, "okay, I've been wanting to actually talk to you, Avery talks about you all the time." And I felt the version slip a little. Because the version I'd picked was for strangers in a low-stakes mode. Jess had just put me in a different mode by saying "Avery talks about you all the time," which meant I was already a character in this room before I walked in, and the character Avery had been describing was probably not the quiet-witty side guy I was performing. The character Avery describes is the one who makes Avery laugh at lying-A side tables. The one Avery says is thoughtful with people.

So there was a half-second where I had to decide — stay in the side-guy version, or try to be the version Avery has apparently been advertising. I tried the second one. I told the dog story. The Sunday-morning dog. The "most alive anyone has been all week" line. Jess laughed in the right place. Then I told the lying-A story and Jess laughed harder, and asked which A had been the lying A, and I said "we never figured out, that's the whole thing." And Jess said, "oh, I get why Avery likes you." Which — okay. That landed. That landed harder than I let myself show. I made a face like "ha, thanks" and took a sip of beer and the conversation moved on, but inside something had — settled, I think is the word.

Because here's what I noticed walking home with Avery later, the air finally cool enough for jackets. The version I reached for at the door wasn't the version Avery had told Jess about. The version I reach for in rooms is always smaller than the version the people who actually know me describe. There's a gap. There's been a gap the whole time. Avery has been telling people I'm thoughtful and funny and Avery's coworker met me and I was performing pleasant-furniture for an hour before letting any of that out. The performance isn't even protective at this point. It's just habitual. The scan happens, the version gets chosen, and the chosen version is always two notches less than what Avery would describe to a stranger about me.

I told Avery, on the walk, that I'd been quiet for a while at the start. Avery said, "yeah, I noticed, you do that." Not as a complaint. As a thing Avery has clocked and accepted. I said, "I think I get scared I'm going to embarrass you." Avery stopped walking for a second and said "babe, no" in the voice. Just that. We kept walking. I held Avery's hand the rest of the way.

I'm not going to fix the scan. I think it's going to keep happening. But Friday for the first time I noticed the gap in real time — not three days later on the floor, in the moment, Jess saying "I get why Avery likes you" — and felt how much of the night I'd spent being a smaller person than I needed to be in a room that would have been fine with the bigger one. Maybe next time I get one notch closer at the door. Maybe I tell the dog story to someone before someone has to invite me to. Maybe.

Plant has seventeen. I don't even know when sixteen happened.

entries.prompt: What's a small thing you do for yourself when no one's watching — a treat, a ritual, a way you spend twenty minutes — that you wouldn't quite want to explain to someone? Tell me the last time you did it and what was going on in the day around it.
entries.content:
Tuesday night. I got home at like 9:40, later than usual, because there'd been a thing at work that ran long and then I'd missed a bus and then walked half the way before catching the next one. Avery wasn't around — Avery had said earlier in the day, "I'm just going to do my own thing tonight" which is Avery's way of saying I need a quiet apartment and you should stay at yours, which I'm fine with, mostly. I'd been a little tightly wound all day. Drew had been short in the 1:1 about something that wasn't actually about me, I think, but I'd taken some of it personally on the elevator down anyway.

So I got home. Dropped the bag. Took the shoes off. And then I did the thing.

The thing is — I read fanfiction. I read fanfiction for a show I haven't actually watched in like three years.

There. I wrote it. The thing I wouldn't quite want to explain.

Specifically — there's a website, and there's a tag, and within that tag there are like four authors I've been following since college, and one of them updates a long-running story sporadically, and I have, over the past two years probably, reread the existing chapters of this one story maybe — eight times? Ten? More than I want to count. It's not even that good objectively. It's a slow-burn coffee-shop AU thing, two characters from a sci-fi show I no longer care about, written by a person whose username is something like "moth_fluorescent" who I imagine is in their thirties, lives somewhere with weather, and cares more about pacing than plot. The chapters are long. The author does this thing where they describe what people are eating in unnecessary detail. There's one chapter, chapter eleven I think, that I have read more than any other piece of writing in my life, including things I had to read for school.

What I did Tuesday — got into bed in my work clothes, didn't even change, opened the laptop, pulled up the bookmark, and reread chapter eleven. Just chapter eleven. About forty minutes. Then closed the laptop and lay there for a while.

That's it. That's the thing.

Why I wouldn't want to explain it. Let me try to actually get at it instead of just gesturing. It's not that fanfiction is embarrassing in some general way — I know intellectually that lots of people read fanfiction, it's a whole thing, there's nothing actually shameful about it. The not-wanting-to-explain is more specific. It's that this particular story, this particular chapter, has become a kind of emotional comfort food I do not have a defensible reason for. The characters are not real. The show is not even one I'd recommend. The author and I have never interacted; I have never left a comment, never even made an account on the site. I am a ghost reader of a half-finished story by a stranger about people who don't exist, and I have spent, cumulatively, probably a full waking day inside chapter eleven over the past two years.

If I tried to tell Avery about it I would have to choose between making it a joke — "haha, I have this dumb fanfiction I reread when I'm sad" — or telling it straight, which would require admitting that there's a paragraph in chapter eleven, near the end, where one character makes the other a sandwich without speaking, and the description of the sandwich-making is somehow the closest thing I have to a stable image of love that I can return to whenever I need one. I cannot say that sentence to another person. I can barely write it here. It sounds, when I write it, both pretentious and pathetic, which is approximately how it feels to be the person who reads it.

What was going on in the day around it. Drew, like I said. The 1:1 thing. Also, on the bus ride home, I'd checked the group chat and there'd been like forty messages I'd missed, Nat and Priya planning something for a weekend in May, and I'd skimmed it and felt the small flat thing where I'm sort of in the chat and sort of not, and didn't reply, and put the phone down. Also I'd eaten badly all day. Coffee, a bagel, a granola bar, nothing that constituted a meal. I was — running on something. Tired in the head. Tired in the chest. The kind of tired where I didn't want to make decisions about what to watch or what to eat or whether to call anyone.

Chapter eleven asks nothing. That's the whole point. I know exactly what's going to happen. I know which lines are coming. I know the sandwich is coming. The sandwich has been coming for two years. There's no scan to run. No version to pick. No grade to receive. It's the textual equivalent of the floor by the chair — a place I go that doesn't require me to be anyone. The fact that the characters aren't real is, I think, part of the comfort. Real people would want something back. Chapter eleven has never asked me a single thing.

After I closed the laptop I made an actual sandwich. Which is funny. I didn't connect those two things until I'm writing it now. I made — peanut butter on bread that was a day past good, one banana sliced on top, the way Mom used to do when I was sick. Ate it in bed. Crumbs everywhere, probably. Brushed at the sheets a little and gave up.

I think the reason I can't quite explain the chapter-eleven thing is that I'd have to admit I have a private inner life that is, in places, kind of childish. That the grown-up me with the job and the relationship and the manager and the calendar still has, parked inside, a person who needs to read the same forty pages over and over to feel okay. That isn't the whole picture of me. But it's a part, and it's the part that runs the controls when I come home tired in my work clothes and don't want to be twenty-four for an hour.

Avery doesn't need to know. Nobody needs to know. Eighteen leaves now. Chapter eleven, again, whenever. Mine.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel relief that something got cancelled — a plan fell through, a meeting got moved, someone bailed? Tell me where you were when you found out and what you actually did with the time that opened up.
entries.content:
Sunday morning, like 9:30. I was still in bed. Avery had texted the night before about brunch with two of Avery's friends — the brunch I'd said yes to on Wednesday in the kind of voice you use to make Wednesday-you sound like a person who likes brunch. I'd been low-grade dreading it since I woke up. Not because the friends are bad. The friends are fine, one of them is the one who tells long stories with good voices, the other one is quieter and I like quiet. The dread was just — a brunch on a Sunday with people who are not Avery and not me, two hours minimum, the order-and-coffee logistics, the bill thing at the end. The whole shape of it.

Then the text came in at 9:34. From Avery. "miguel's stomach is doing a thing, they're bailing, ok if we just do something chill?" Three sentences. The relief was — I want to be honest about how big it was, which is embarrassing. It was disproportionate. My whole chest unclenched. I felt my shoulders drop. I texted back "oh no poor him" and then, in a separate bubble, "yeah totally chill is good." Two messages. The "oh no poor him" first because I knew if I sent the relieved one first I'd feel weird about it, and front-loading the concern was a small way of laundering the response.

Miguel's stomach. Bless Miguel's stomach. I am genuinely sorry Miguel's stomach is doing a thing. I am also profoundly grateful to it.

What I did with the time. Okay. The plan would've had me out the door by 10:30, on the train by 11, eating eggs by 11:30, paying around 1, home around 2. So roughly four hours opened up. The first thing I did was — nothing. I lay in bed. I lay in bed for a real stretch, like another forty minutes, with the phone face-down on the nightstand, just looking at the ceiling. Italy's still up there. I noticed for the first time that there's another smaller stain near it that's vaguely shaped like — I don't know, a dog? Some kind of animal. Italy and a dog. The whole room, slowly, accumulating geography I haven't logged before.

Then I made coffee, the bad kind, and stood at the counter. The plant has nineteen leaves now, I checked twice. Then I did the thing where you walk around your apartment in a t-shirt holding coffee and look at it like a stranger, like you're considering buying it. I noticed the chair pile. I noticed I'd left a glass on the bookshelf at some point. I noticed the kitchen rug is filthy. I did not do anything about any of these things. Just noticed. It was the most leisurely audit I've ever done of my own apartment because nothing was waiting on the other end of it.

Around 11:15 I went outside. Not anywhere. Just out. The weather had done that thing again, the orange light but in morning form, soft, and I walked four blocks to the not-favorite coffee place — not the Kyle one, a different one, the one where I'm a stranger — and got a real coffee and a pastry. I sat on a bench outside and ate the pastry slowly, which is something I never do. I usually eat pastries while walking, while answering Slack, while doing something else. Just sitting on a bench with a pastry felt almost transgressive. A guy walked by with a small dog wearing a sweater and the dog looked at me like it had questions and I said "I know, buddy" out loud, to the dog, which is a thing I would never do in front of anyone.

What I keep thinking about, sitting here writing about it, is — the relief was not really about the brunch. The brunch would've been fine. I'd have rallied. I'd have done the version, asked-good-questions-Kai, complimented-the-eggs-Kai, would have gotten through it and probably even enjoyed parts of it. The relief was about not having to do the version. About getting four hours back where I didn't have to be a particular shape for anyone. That's what got cancelled. Not eggs. The shape.

And what's bothering me a little — or not bothering, just sitting next to me — is that I get this relief a lot. Meetings cancelled, plans fell through, the friend who flakes. Every time, the relief. Which means a lot of my life is, apparently, being scheduled with things I'm secretly hoping won't happen. Which is a weird way to live. I don't think the answer is to stop saying yes to brunch. Some brunches are good. Some brunches I've ended up liking. The Jess thing, in the end, was good once I let it be. The answer is probably more like — to notice that the thing I want, when things get cancelled, is just an unstructured stretch. And maybe I could give myself those without waiting for someone's stomach to bail me out. Just — block off a Sunday morning sometimes. Not as a treat. As a basic input.

I went back home around 12:30. Avery came over around 2. We didn't do much. We sat on the couch and Avery worked on something on a laptop and I read a book — an actual book, not chapter eleven, a real book I've been forty pages into for two months — and I read another thirty pages. Avery's foot was on my leg. The radiator was clicking. Around 4 we made grilled cheese.

I texted Miguel later, through Avery, "feel better!" I meant it, mostly. I owed him.

Plant has nineteen. I'm losing count of what was when. The relief is still in me a little, somehow. Nineteen leaves and a Sunday I didn't have to perform.

entries.prompt: Tell me about a recent time you felt yourself working harder than the moment required — overexplaining something, over-preparing for a small thing, putting more effort into a text than it needed. What were you actually trying to head off?
entries.content:
Wednesday. The email to Drew about the offsite.

There's a team offsite next month, two days, just our team plus a couple people from the other team. Drew sent a Slack last week asking if anyone wanted to lead a working session — pick a topic, run an hour, basically a thing where you stand up and walk people through something. Optional. Lowercase ask. Nobody had volunteered yet when I saw it, and after sitting with it for a day I decided to say I'd do it. I'd do the cross-team dependency thing. The diagram thing, basically, but the bigger version of it, the one where I actually walk people through how to think about it instead of just drawing one on a whiteboard in the moment.

So Wednesday morning I went to email Drew about it. Just to say yes, here's what I'd want to do, anything you want me to know.

Three sentences. That's all it needed to be. Maybe four. "Hey — happy to take a working session if it's still open. Was thinking about doing something on cross-team dependency mapping, building on the timeline doc. Let me know if that lands or if you'd rather I pick something else."

That's the email. That's a real, complete email. A sane person sends that email in ninety seconds and goes back to work.

I spent forty-five minutes on it.

I wrote a first draft that was three paragraphs. Three paragraphs. The first paragraph was a kind of throat-clear about the offsite generally, like I needed to establish that I had read the original Slack carefully. The second paragraph was the actual offer. The third paragraph was a list of three possible angles — dependency mapping, the framing thing Reese had used, or some kind of retro on the timeline project — with little parentheticals about which one I thought made the most sense and why. There was a sentence at the end that said something like "totally happy to defer to your sense of what'd be most useful for the group." Defer. I used the word defer. In an email about whether I could do an optional working session.

I read the draft and even I could tell it was too much. I cut the first paragraph. I cut the third option from the list. I cut "defer." I rewrote the parentheticals. Then I added a different sentence at the end about how I'd been thinking about this stuff anyway because of the cross-team meeting, which was a kind of bid to make the offer sound natural and not like I'd been hovering over the Slack message for a day deciding whether to reach for it. Then I cut that sentence because it made the offer sound less natural, not more. Then I rewrote the opening, three times, to land somewhere between casual and serious. Then I read the whole thing out loud at my desk, very quietly, to see if the rhythm worked. Then I changed "happy to take a working session" to "would be up for taking a working session" because "happy" sounded too eager and then changed it back because "would be up for" sounded too cool, like I was doing Drew a favor.

Forty-five minutes. For an email that, when I finally sent it, was four sentences long and nearly identical to the version I could have sent before I made coffee.

Drew replied within ten minutes. The reply said: "yeah, that sounds great. dependency mapping works. send me an outline whenever, no rush." Two sentences. Lowercase. The reply took, generously, twelve seconds to compose.

I sat at my desk after, kind of laughing at myself in the small interior way. Forty-five minutes of work on my side. Twelve seconds on Drew's. I had treated the email like a deliverable. Drew had treated it like a Slack.

What was I actually trying to head off. Let me try.

The surface answer is — I was trying to make sure the offer sounded competent. That if Drew was going to think about whether to let me lead a session, the email itself would already be evidence that I could lead a session. Like the email was an audition for the thing. Which it kind of was, in my head. Drew has never seen me lead anything in front of a group. The diagram was four people in a room. The doc was alone at my desk. A working session is on my feet, in front of the team, for a full hour, with slides probably, with questions. Drew had to decide if that was a thing I could do. So the email had to be — already that thing, a little. Polished, organized, three options offered, defer. The shape of competence packed into four sentences disguised as casual.

But underneath that. The deeper thing. I think I was trying to head off the version where I said yes to the working session and Drew, very gently, said back "actually, let's have someone else take this one." I was trying to make the email so airtight that there was no opening for that. If I gave Drew three options, Drew would feel locked into picking one of them, and the meta-question of whether I should be doing this at all would never get raised. The over-preparation was a way of not letting Drew reconsider. It was, in a small way, manipulation — gentle, polite, but real. I was trying to corner Drew into yes by making no awkward to deliver.

Which Drew presumably didn't notice. Drew got the email, read about half of it probably, and said yeah great. Drew was not auditioning the email. Drew was not trying to figure out whether I should lead. Drew had asked the original question with no preferred answer and was basically just relieved someone said yes.

The other thing I was trying to head off, sitting with it now — fear of the offsite itself. The session is in three weeks. I have three weeks now to over-prepare in earnest. Forty-five minutes on the email is, I'm realizing, a tiny preview of the next twenty-one days, in which I will rehearse the working session in the shower, on the bus, in bed at 1am, until the actual hour arrives and I either deliver the rehearsed version cleanly and feel hollow, or I improvise in the moment and feel scrappy and human, the way the cadence ask went. The over-preparation isn't just about Drew's reception. It's about my own dread of standing up in front of people and not being polished. The email was a tiny pre-payment on that dread. Forty-five minutes of doing-something to manage forty-five seconds of being-watched, three weeks from now.

I'm going to make the outline this weekend. Real outline. Not slides yet. Not rehearsals. Just the bones. We'll see what I do with the bones.

Plant has twenty. Even.

entries.prompt: When did someone last ask you for something — a favor, your time, an opinion — and you said yes faster than you'd actually decided? Walk me through the moment they asked and what was running underneath the yes.
entries.content:
Yesterday. The kitchen at work, around 3:15. I was getting water — I'm always getting water, the water trips are basically punctuation in my day at this point — and Mara from the other team came in. The same Mara from the lunch a few months ago, the one who'd asked what people did over the weekend. We've talked maybe four times total. She's nice. Quick smile, talks fast, has the kind of presence where she leans in a little when she asks you something.

She said, "oh, perfect, I was going to Slack you. Could you take a look at a doc I'm putting together for next week? Just like, your eyes on it, your dependency-mapping brain. Doesn't have to be deep."

I said "yeah, totally, send it over."

That was it. The whole transaction took maybe nine seconds. She said thanks, said she'd send it after she got back from a thing, refilled her water bottle, left. I stood there with my own water glass and felt the small late-arriving "wait" that always comes about three steps behind the yes.

Because — okay. "Just your eyes on it, doesn't have to be deep." I know what that means. I've been on the other side of it. That phrase is what you say when you want a real review but don't want to be the kind of person who asks for a real review. The doc is going to be long. The dependency stuff is the hardest part of the doc, which is why she's asking. "Doesn't have to be deep" means she'd love deep if I have deep in me. And of course I have deep in me, because I'm me, and I can't half-read a doc someone has handed me with their name on it. So the actual cost of yes is, conservatively, an hour, probably more like ninety minutes if I'm being honest, plus whatever follow-up Slack thread comes after. And I have the working session outline I told myself I'd start this weekend. And I'm already behind on a thing of my own that's due Tuesday. And Avery and I had said we'd do something Saturday morning that I haven't planned.

None of this was in my head when I said yes. The yes was already out before any of that math got to do its math. The math is doing its math now, in the kitchen, with a water glass.

What was running underneath the yes. Let me try.

First thing — Mara asked specifically. "Your dependency-mapping brain." Not "anyone with bandwidth," not a general ask. She'd thought about who to ask and the answer was me. That's a kind of thing I'm not used to and apparently haven't built any tolerance for. The second she named the thing I'm allegedly good at, my mouth was already saying yes, because saying no would be a kind of betrayal of the version of me she was describing. If I said "actually I'm slammed this week, can it wait" the implicit message would be "you've miscast me, I'm not actually that person." Which is a thing I would rather work an extra ninety minutes than say.

Second thing — and this is uglier — I was a little flattered that she asked. Specifically flattered. Mara is on the other team, she's adjacent to Drew without being under Drew, which means a good review from me on her doc is a thing that exists in a part of the company where Drew might hear about it secondhand. I noticed this thought in the kitchen, after the fact, and felt slightly gross about it. Because the calculation is: Mara asked, doing this well is a small positive token in the ledger, the ledger is the one Drew might one day reference, therefore yes. I would like to think I said yes because I'm a generous coworker. I think I said yes because I'm a generous coworker AND because some part of me is still treating every interaction like an audition that gets reported back to the head judge.

Third thing, underneath both of those — I cannot, apparently, sit with even a half-second of someone wanting something from me and not getting it. The shape of the ask was Mara, leaning in slightly, with a small "hopeful" energy. To say "let me check my week" — which is the actual sane response, the response Avery would give, the response a normal person would give — I would have had to let the hopeful energy hang in the air for two beats while I thought. And the hanging is the thing I can't do. I'd rather absorb the work than let someone hold their want unanswered in front of me. That's the real reflex. Not yes-to-Mara. Yes-to-anyone-who-needs-something-right-now.

It's the same shape as the barista. The marriage joke. The "totally great" to Dad. The version that costs the other person nothing. I have a whole department in my head devoted to making sure no one ever has to wait for an answer or hold an awkward moment. I am extremely well-staffed in that department, at the cost of every other department.

What I'm sitting with now — Mara hasn't sent the doc yet. There's a window. I could Slack her and say "hey, just realized I'm tighter this week than I thought, would Monday work, or do you need it sooner?" That's not a no. It's a renegotiation. It's the thing I should have said in the kitchen if I'd had a half-second of friction between the ask and my mouth.

I might do it. I'm writing it here so I have to look at it. The half-second of friction is the same trick as the phone face-down. I don't get the friction in the moment yet. But I get it in the kitchen afterward, with the water glass, and maybe afterward is enough to send the Slack before the doc even arrives.

Plant has twenty-one. The trick keeps being the same trick. I keep being slightly later to it than I want to be, and slightly earlier than I used to be.

entries.prompt:
The last three entries have all been about the same beat — saying yes too fast, working too hard at small asks, the relief when something gets cancelled. Time to come at the corpus from a different angle. Recent prompts have leaned heavily on "tell me about a time when…" — let me try a different opener and a different domain. Something about a person who's been on the edges, or a moment of unexpected warmth, or anger, or a small private win. Anger hasn't come up since late March. Neither has admiration. Let me try admiration with a twist — when you noticed someone doing something well and it stirred something in you.

Who's someone you've watched recently — a co
entries.content:
Drew. Which is — yeah. I know. I keep writing about Drew. But this is a different angle, I think, or at least it felt different watching it.

Tuesday all-hands. Reese wasn't presenting this time. Drew was. Drew almost never presents, normally Drew just unmutes for a comment or types in chat, but there was a thing the leadership wanted communicated and Drew was the one who got handed it. And the thing they wanted communicated was — I don't want to be specific because the specifics aren't mine — but it was bad. Or not bad exactly. Disappointing. A direction the team had been counting on that wasn't going to happen the way it had been described. The kind of news where everyone in the call is going to feel something, and where the person delivering it gets to choose whether to cushion it or be honest about it or do the corporate thing where you reframe the loss as an exciting pivot.

Drew did none of those things. Drew just — said it. Plainly. Said this is what's happening, said here's why, said I know this is going to be frustrating and I'd be frustrated too. Then took questions. Real questions, not softball ones. Someone — I think Marcus, actually, the same Marcus from the diagram meeting — asked a question that was barely a question, more of a complaint with an upturn at the end, and Drew just said "yeah, that's fair" and answered the part underneath the complaint instead of getting defensive about the complaint itself. Someone else asked a thing Drew didn't know the answer to, and Drew said "I don't know, I'll find out." Just like that. No filler. No "great question." No buying time.

I was watching from my desk and I felt something I haven't really felt about Drew before. Not the four-hundred-pound thing. Not the screenshot thing. Not the away-light thing. I felt — admiration. Clean. Just, oh. That's a person being good at a hard part of their job. That's a person not flinching when flinching is the available move.

And then the thing I want to write about, the thing that stirred. After the call I sat there and I noticed I wasn't running my usual program. I wasn't doing the "what does this mean about me" math. I wasn't checking whether Drew had looked at my video square at any point. I was just thinking about what Drew had done. Which is — that's a different relationship to a person than I've had with Drew the whole time I've been at this job. For nine months Drew has been a mirror I'm trying to read. For thirty minutes on Tuesday Drew was just a person I was watching do a thing well.

The stir was — I want to be that. Not the manager part. Not the senior part. The part where you say the actual sentence in the actual room without dressing it up. "Yeah, that's fair." "I don't know, I'll find out." Those are such small sentences. They are the sentences I have spent forty-five minutes trying to avoid in emails. They are the sentences a person with nothing to prove can say easily and a person who is auditioning constantly cannot say at all. Drew has, presumably, gotten to a place where Drew can say "I don't know" out loud to forty people on a video call without feeling like the floor is dropping out. That's not a personality trait. That's a thing that got built.

Which means it's buildable. Which is the part that kept me at my desk for a while after.

I tried to picture myself doing the equivalent. Not the all-hands version. The smaller version. Mara asks me a question about her doc next week and I don't know the answer and instead of bluffing or hedging or over-explaining I just say "I don't know, let me look." Or Drew asks me something in the 1:1 and instead of giving the prepared anecdote I say "honestly, I'm not sure, I've been thinking about it but I haven't landed." Or Avery asks about long term and I don't crack a marriage joke, I just say "yes, I think about it, and I get scared." Same shape. The just-saying-the-thing shape. Drew did it in front of forty people. I can't do it in front of one.

But — okay. The honest thing. I sort of did it with Avery on Friday. I told Avery about the embarrassing-Avery scan-fear on the walk home and Avery stopped walking. That was a small "I don't know, let me find out" — I didn't have the polished version, I just said the messy thing. And I sort of did it in the 1:1 with the cadence ask. The "growing in the role" instead of "developing." Scrappy. Mine. So the muscle exists. It's small. Drew's is bigger because Drew has been using it longer. That's all that is. It's not a different species of person. It's the same muscle at different sizes.

What's surprising me a little, writing this — I don't feel envy at Drew the way I felt the flash at Ben. With Ben it was the direction. With Drew it's the bearing. And the bearing doesn't feel like something I'm losing by not having yet. It feels like something I'm slowly acquiring. Drew on Tuesday was a preview, maybe. Of what it looks like when you've been doing the small version for a while and the muscle has grown into the bigger version. I'm — maybe a year in, on the small version. Drew is presumably ten or fifteen.

The other thing. I want to write this even though it's a little tender. I think part of why the four hundred pounds has come down is that I'm starting to see Drew as a person I could learn from instead of a person I have to perform for. Those are different relationships and only one of them is sustainable. The first one I can be in for years. The second one was going to break me eventually.

Plant has twenty-two. Drew said "I don't know" out loud and I'm still thinking about it three days later. Different kind of replay than the screenshot kind. Better kind.

entries.prompt: Think about a place in your apartment you've been spending more time in lately — a chair, a window, the kitchen counter, the floor. Walk me through the last time you were there and what was running through your head.
entries.content:
The kitchen counter. Not the floor by the chair, not anymore, or not as much. The kitchen counter, specifically the corner of it by the window where the plant lives, where I can stand with my hands on the edge of it and look out at the alley and the brick wall of the building next door and the slice of sky above the brick.

Last night, around 7:30. Avery wasn't over, which has stopped being a thing I narrate to myself as alone, it's just — Avery's at Avery's, I'm at mine, we'll see each other Friday. I'd gotten home and made actual dinner, which is becoming a thing I do sometimes now, not always but more than zero — pasta with whatever was in the fridge, garlic and the end of a lemon and the parmesan that's been there too long but isn't actually too long. Ate at the counter standing up, which is what I do when I eat alone, because sitting at the table by myself feels weirdly formal. Then I rinsed the plate and instead of going to the couch I just stayed there. Hands on the edge of the counter. Looking out the window.

The plant has twenty-three leaves. I know because I counted while I was standing there, again, like I always do. The light at 7:30 right now is doing the thing where it goes through the window at a low angle and lights up the leaves from behind so you can see the veins. I had not really registered that plants have veins until this one started showing them to me. The sky above the brick was that color it gets when it's not quite done with the day. Some kid was yelling about something two buildings over, the kind of yelling that's actually playing.

What was running through my head — okay, the honest version. I was thinking about the working session. Three weeks out now, two and a half. I have the bones of an outline. I have not made slides. I have, twice, lain in bed at 1am rehearsing the opening sentence of the session in my head, which is exactly the thing I told myself I would not do for three weeks straight. So the outline-in-bed thing has started, despite the writing-it-down warning, despite knowing better. The audition muscle is louder than the noticing muscle still. Some of the time. Most of the time, even.

But standing at the counter, the rehearsing kind of — went down a notch. Not because I made it. It just got quieter on its own when I stopped looking at the phone or the laptop and started looking at the brick. Something about a flat brown wall and a slice of sky doesn't take a rehearsal. The rehearsal needs an audience, even an imagined one, and the wall isn't an audience. The wall is just a wall.

I started thinking, then, about how I've been ending up here a lot. The counter, this exact spot, the hands-on-the-edge spot. Not for long. Just five or ten minutes at a time, before something or after something. Coffee in the morning sometimes, instead of taking the cup back to bed. After dinner sometimes, like last night. Once at 11pm with a glass of water I didn't really need, just to be standing somewhere that wasn't the bed or the couch. It's becoming a place. I have not consciously decided to make it a place. It just keeps being where I end up when I don't have anywhere to be.

What's different about the counter from the floor by the chair — I think the floor was for waiting things out. For when something was running and I needed to let it run. The counter is for — I don't know. Looking. Just looking. The plant is right there at eye level when I'm leaning, and the window is right there, and there's nothing to do at the counter, you can't really do anything at a counter while standing at it without a project. So it's a place I go when I want to not be doing.

That feels new. Not the having-of-the-place. The wanting-to-not-be-doing. Six months ago — three months ago — not-doing felt like failing. The thing on Sunday morning when I rewatched the bakery show was the first time I noticed the guilt voice not catching, and I wrote about it like it was a small revelation. The counter is, I think, the slow-cooked version of that. A small place in the apartment that's organized around the principle that I do not have to be producing or processing or improving for ten minutes after dinner. I can just stand there. The plant doesn't need anything from me. The wall doesn't either. The kid yelling outside doesn't.

I noticed, while I was standing there, that I was breathing differently. Slower. I don't know if I was breathing differently because I was calm or if I was calm because I was breathing differently. I'm not going to figure that out. But I noticed it, and the noticing didn't make me start performing the breathing, which I have a bad habit of doing the second I become aware of breathing — start managing it, start making it look like the breathing of a calm person. I just kept breathing the way I'd been breathing. The wall stayed brown. The leaves stayed lit.

After maybe ten minutes I got a text from Hannah. A picture of her dog wearing a hat. No caption. I laughed and sent back a picture of the plant. No caption. We do this now, apparently. It's become a thing. Pictures, no captions, no expectations. The friendship resumed at the level of "shoe."

Then I did go to the couch. Read more of the book. Thirty more pages. The book is going to get finished eventually. Probably not soon. That's fine. The counter, the plant, the brick, the slow breathing. Twenty-three leaves and a place to stand.

entries.prompt: Tell me about a time this week you were around someone — a friend, a family member, anyone — and caught yourself performing a version of being okay that was a little ahead of where you actually were. What did they see, and what was underneath it?
entries.content:
Sunday afternoon. FaceTime with Mom and Dad, both of them this time, which is rare — usually it's Mom on Tuesday and Dad's voice from the kitchen. But Sunday they were both on the couch in the den, both in the frame, Dad in his Sunday t-shirt, Mom with her glasses pushed up on her head, the way she does when she's been reading.

They called me. That's also rare. Usually I call, or Mom calls, but this was both of them, together, on a Sunday afternoon, which made my stomach do a small thing before I picked up. Like — what's this. Is something wrong. Is somebody sick. I picked up bracing.

Nothing was wrong. They'd just — gotten home from brunch with friends, and the friends had asked about me, and that had apparently set off some kind of we-should-call-Kai impulse, and here we were. Mom said "we just wanted to see your face." Dad said "yeah, hi sweetie." Both of them looking at the camera at slightly different angles because Mom was holding the phone and Dad was leaning in.

And I — okay. I want to write this carefully because the performance was so smooth I almost missed it. They asked how I was. I said "good, really good actually, busy week but good." Then I told them about the working session at the offsite. I'd been planning to mention it on Tuesday, the next call, but they asked, and out it came. I told them I was going to lead a session. I told them Drew had said yes immediately. I told them I'd been thinking about the structure. I made it sound — I made it sound like a thing I was excited about. Which, on some level, I am. But the version I gave them was 90% excited and 10% nervous, and the actual ratio in my body is more like 40% excited and 60% the kind of low-grade dread that's been making me rehearse the opening sentence in bed at 1am.

Mom said "oh sweetie, I'm so proud of you." Dad said "that's great, kiddo, that's really great." And I felt — okay, this is the part. I felt the warmth of them being proud, and at the same time I felt a small private flinch, because the thing they were proud of was a slightly inflated version. They were proud of someone who was excited about a working session. The actual someone is excited and also kind of secretly hoping it gets cancelled. The Miguel's-stomach version. If Drew slacked tomorrow saying "hey, we're cutting working sessions from the offsite, going to keep it lighter" I would feel relief disproportionate to the news. They don't know that. I performed the excited version for them and they took it at face value and now I have to maintain that performance for the next three weeks until the session happens, plus on the call after, where I will tell them how it went, and the version I give them then will also be edited.

What did they see. They saw a daughter — son — kid, whatever, they saw their kid doing well at a job in a city across the country and they got to feel, for a few minutes on a Sunday after brunch, that the worry they carry around about me was misplaced. Mom got to relax her shoulders. I could see it on the screen. Dad got to nod and say "that's great, kiddo" twice, which is a lot for him. They got the version they needed and I delivered it.

What was underneath. A bunch of things, layered. The thing right at the top — I'm tired. Like, body-tired. The week had been long, the offsite is taking up real estate, I'd been on the floor by the chair Thursday night for the first time in a while, looping about the working session and whether I'd asked for it because I wanted to do it or because I wanted to be the kind of person who'd asked for it, which are different. Underneath the tired — the question of the savings, which I still haven't actually engaged with, just briefly logged into the once. The plant has twenty-three leaves and the savings number has been the same number since I peeked at it two months ago, which I haven't told them because the answer to "have you been putting away?" is "I have a thing set up, I don't really know" and that is not an answer Mom can hold. Underneath that — the fact that the working session is a real audition, of a real kind, in front of real people, and I'm scared. Not of bombing exactly. Of being mediocre. Of the room going polite and Drew saying "good" the way Drew says good when something was fine and unmemorable.

I didn't say any of that. I said busy week but good. I said the offsite. I said Drew said yes. I asked them about brunch. Mom told a story about her friend's daughter who's having a baby. I said "oh wow, give her my best." Dad mentioned the car needing tires again, like it had the last time, and I almost laughed because the tires have apparently entered the recurring-segment phase of his conversational repertoire.

The thing I keep landing on, sitting on the couch after — the performance wasn't entirely a lie. I am excited about the session. I do think I can do it. The 40% is real. I just gave them the 90% version because the 60% nervous would have made Mom call her sister about me, and Dad would have said "are you sure you should be doing it" in his concerned voice, and I would have spent the rest of the week managing their concern from a thousand miles away on top of managing the actual session.

So the performance is — protective. Of them. Of me. Of the call staying short and warm. But it has a cost, which is that the version of me they're proud of is partly a costume, and I can feel the small ache of being praised for the costume instead of the person inside it. Which is, I think, why I can't quite take the praise from Avery cleanly either. Different version, same ache.

After we hung up I stood at the counter for a while. The brick was doing its evening thing. The plant has twenty-three leaves still, no new ones since Thursday. I made tea. I didn't text anyone. I just stood there and let the after of the call settle. They saw a kid who's doing well. The kid is doing okay. Most days. Today, even. The okay just isn't the shape they were applauding, and I didn't have the energy to redirect.

Maybe Tuesday. Maybe I tell Mom the 60%. Maybe I don't. The tea was chamomile again. Twenty-three.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel a flicker of resentment toward someone close to you — not a blowup, just a small inward pull-back? Tell me where you were, what they did or said, and what you did with it in the minutes after.
entries.content:
Friday night. Avery's place. We'd ordered the Thai, the same Thai, the place that always forgets the spring rolls and we always notice and never call about it. We were on the couch. Avery had the laptop open and was — kind of working, kind of not, doing the thing where Avery answers Slacks with one hand and feeds me pad see ew with the other, which is normally fine. It is normally fine. We've done it a hundred times. Friday-evening overflow, Avery's laptop glowing, my legs across Avery's lap, the show on but not loud.

Avery said, in the middle of all this, "oh, I told Jess we'd do dinner with her and Sam next Thursday, that works right?"

Past tense. Told. Already a yes on the calendar.

And I felt it. Right under the breastbone. A small inward pull-back, like something had stepped half a foot away from Avery without my body actually moving. I said "yeah, sure, sounds good" and reached for my water and the rest of the sentence I almost said — "would've been nice to be asked first" — stayed in my mouth as a small bitter thing I swallowed down with the water.

Avery didn't notice. Or noticed and didn't push, which is the more likely thing because Avery notices most stuff. Avery just went back to the Slack. The show kept going. I kept eating. The pull-back stayed, this small cool spot in the middle of an otherwise warm couch.

Why. The Jess thing on its own is nothing. Jess is fine. I liked Jess. Sam I haven't met but Avery says I'll like Sam. A Thursday dinner is a Thursday dinner. I would have said yes if Avery had asked. I would have said yes faster than I'd actually decided, even, the way I always do. So it isn't really about whether I want to go.

It's that Avery told Jess yes for me without checking. Which means Avery is, on some small level, deciding which version of me shows up where, on what nights. Which is — it's not unfair. I do this too. I commit us to my coworker's whatever. I say yes for both of us when someone asks. We both do it. It's how the relationship works. But Friday on the couch I felt the small flicker of — Avery has picked up that I will say yes. Avery has, maybe, started routing around the asking because the asking is a formality, because the answer is always yes. And the version of me that always says yes is a version I've been writing about not wanting to be, and it stung a little to feel it being assumed by the person who knows me best.

That was the actual thing. Not Jess. The assumed yes.

Underneath that, smaller, uglier — the offsite is in a week and a half. I have been stretched a little thin. I have been rehearsing the opening sentence in bed. And what I really wanted from Friday night was to not have anything else added to the week ahead, even a nice thing, even a Thursday dinner I'll probably enjoy. The pull-back was partly resentment that Avery hadn't somehow known the week ahead was already loaded, despite me not having said the week ahead was loaded, despite me having performed the 40-percent-excited version of the offsite to Avery the same way I performed the 90-percent-excited version to Mom and Dad. I had given Avery a clean read on me and was now annoyed Avery hadn't read between lines I hadn't drawn.

Which is — yeah. I see it. I wrote that and saw it. The resentment was at Avery for not telepathically catching the thing I had actively edited out.

What I did with it in the minutes after. Sat with it. The pull-back didn't go away just because I clocked it. I kept eating, kept watching, but I was running a small parallel track for maybe ten minutes where I cataloged other times Avery had committed us to something without asking. The list was short. Embarrassingly short. Three, maybe four times in eight months. And in two of those I had actually said the thing — "hey, can you check with me before saying yes" — and Avery had said sure and meant it. So the catalog wasn't really evidence of a pattern. The catalog was the resentment looking for a structure to live in.

Around the second commercial break I did a thing that's new for me, kind of. I said it. Not big. I said, "hey — small thing, but next time can you ask me before saying yes? Not about Jess specifically, that's fine, just in general." Avery looked up from the laptop. Actually looked, closed the laptop a little. Said, "oh — yeah, sorry, I just — yeah, you're right. I'll ask." Not defensive. Not effusive. Just yeah. And then, "are you good with Thursday or do you want me to push it?"

I said good with Thursday. I meant it. The pull-back loosened almost immediately, which surprised me, because usually these things calcify in me for hours and I write about them later from the floor. This one came out at the second commercial break and dissolved by the third. The cool spot warmed. Avery's hand was on my ankle again. The spring rolls were still missing. We laughed about that, eventually, the way we always do.

What I'm sitting with — I've been writing for weeks about not letting wants stay in my mouth. Drew got the Slack. Hannah got the photo. Ben got the apology. Avery got, last night, a fifteen-second sentence about being asked first. That one was the smallest of them and might be the most ordinary win. The kind of thing functional people say to their partners on couches all the time. I just got to be one of them, briefly, on a Friday, with pad see ow on my chest and the resentment going down to nothing the second I said the actual thing instead of swallowing it.

Plant has twenty-four. I noticed when I got home Saturday morning. The week is loaded and the dinner is on the calendar and the asking-first will probably need to be asked-for again, and that's fine. Twenty-four.

parts.name: The Auditioner
parts.description: Rehearses, scans faces, pre-loads takes, and performs polished versions of self to win approval—especially from authority figures.

parts.name: The Avoider
parts.description: The fast flinch — opens a new tab, looks away from doorways, lets messages sit unread, finds the next sandwich video.

parts.name: The Comforter
parts.description: Self-soothes through low-demand rituals — Thai takeout, the bakery show, chapter eleven, the fuck-it voice — when scanning has run the system tired.

parts.name: The Deflater
parts.description: Keeps a rigged internal ledger — replays mistakes for weeks, dissolves wins within an hour, and finds the angle by which any good moment wasn't really that good.

parts.name: The Holder
parts.description: The waiting-out place — sits on the floor or at the counter, watches the plant, lets loops run until tired without managing or fixing.

parts.name: The Quiet One
parts.description: Carries the loneliness of let-go ropes — friendships gone dormant, the un-said "I miss you," the version of self that existed before the scanning began.

parts.name: The Small One
parts.description: Carries the feeling of being a child in a costume — inadequate, thin-scaffolded, not yet a real adult, undone by unexpected kindness.

parts.name: The Smoother
parts.description: Says yes faster than decided, edits answers to cost others nothing, and absorbs awkwardness rather than letting another person hold an unanswered want.
